Does an increase in horsepower explain a rise in exhaust temperature?
October 1, 2011
Question: I have a 1989 38-foot Bayliner with twin 175-hp Hino diesels that have been retrofitted with turbochargers, thus raising each engine to 210 horsepower. As I go from cruise to wide-open throttle (approximately 23 mph at 2800 rpm), turbo exhaust temperatures increase from 700F to 950F on the port engine and 850F on the starboard. When
Can switching motor oil viscosity harm my engine?
September 30, 2011
Question: The boys at the Ko Olina harbor are having a heated discussion concerning motor oil. Since Costco hit town, we’ve all been able to buy Chevron 15W-40 Delo 400 heavy-duty motor oil at ridiculously low prices. But what about the older engines we’ve been using straight 40W Delo 400 and other single-viscosity products in for years? Can we
Using synthetic oils in older engines
August 1, 2011
Question: I own a 1996 43-foot Hatteras with twin six-cylinder Detroit Diesel engines. I change the oil approximately every 100 hours of operation. At this point, I have 1,200 hours on each diesel and so far have only used non-synthetic oil. Is a synthetic or a blend of synthetic and regular oil contraindicated for this aged engine for any
Vintage Yanmars Running Hot
June 1, 2011
Question: I have a 29 Phoenix with twin, 1984-vintage, 170-hp Yanmar diesels with 1,000 hours on them. While the boat seems to run fine, I have one issue. During operation, the temperature of each engine rises normally at first but then keeps on going, not to the point of setting off alarms, but close. Pegging the engines (i.e., throttling them
Idling diesel engines
May 1, 2011
Question: My mechanic friends tell me not to let my diesel engine idle for long. They say that it should either be under load or turned off. I hear a lot of diesel engines in trucks idling at truck stops for hours though. Is there a difference in the two types of diesel engines or are my mechanic friends mistaken

April 1, 2011
Question: I have a 1990 Carver Yacht with two 375-hp Caterpillar 3208 diesels. The starboard engine works fine, but at 2000 rpm the port engine overheats. Once the temperature hits 200F, I slow down to prevent damage and everything returns to normal.
Seasonal Storage For Fuel Tanks
March 1, 2011
Question: When I took your one-day Introductory Diesel Maintenance Course several years ago, I forgot to ask one question: What is the best way to lay up a fuel tank for seasonal storage? For years now, I’ve been topping off my tanks when fall lay-up comes around in order to reduce the air space above the fuel and thus minimize algae growth. Just

And since I bought my boat some nine years ago, I have
Caterpillar 3208 low rpm

Anyway, I determined shortly thereafter that the gasket had blown into the cylinder although no cooling fluid had escaped. So I hired a diesel shop to

A year ago, I cleaned the heat exchanger and installed a new raw-water pump. There are no overheating issues. Do I have an injector or rack problem?—Charlie Carpenter, Morristown, New
Noisy Engines
November 1, 2010
Question: I have a pair of Detroit Diesel 6-71 TIBs in my 1990 Ocean 48 Super Sport. I love both the boat and the motors, but the Detroits have always been loud at the exhaust end of things. As I grow older, this noise is starting to bother me, particularly at times when I have to shout to be heard on the flying bridge at cruise speed. Muffler companies tell me that modern

Caring for engines after a not-too-long hiatus.
How do I check for engines damaged by forgetting to open seacocks?
I left my marina with my seacocks closed…

Should we do end-of-season oil changes or can we stretch the old oil into the next season?

I plan on being away from my boat for two to three months and during that time, I plan on having someone periodically run my diesels dockside…

Question: I have a 1989 38-foot Bayliner with twin 175-hp Hino diesels that have been retrofitted with turbochargers, thus raising each engine to 210 horsepower. As I go from cruise to wide-open throttle (approximately 23 mph at 2800 rpm), turbo exhaust temperatures increase from 700F to 950F on the port engine and 850F on the starboard. When
Can switching motor oil viscosity harm my engine?
Question: The boys at the Ko Olina harbor are having a heated discussion concerning motor oil. Since Costco hit town, we’ve all been able to buy Chevron 15W-40 Delo 400 heavy-duty motor oil at ridiculously low prices. But what about the older engines we’ve been using straight 40W Delo 400 and other single-viscosity products in for years? Can we
Using synthetic oils in older engines
Question: I own a 1996 43-foot Hatteras with twin six-cylinder Detroit Diesel engines. I change the oil approximately every 100 hours of operation. At this point, I have 1,200 hours on each diesel and so far have only used non-synthetic oil. Is a synthetic or a blend of synthetic and regular oil contraindicated for this aged engine for any
Vintage Yanmars Running Hot
Question: I have a 29 Phoenix with twin, 1984-vintage, 170-hp Yanmar diesels with 1,000 hours on them. While the boat seems to run fine, I have one issue. CraigCat Adventure Catamaran
November 8, 2024
At the boat shows, we’re always on the lookout for unique vessels. Invented by Erik and Robert Craig nearly 30 years ago and refined through all those years, these speedy little 30-horsepower, joystick-steered catamarans feature super-stable and tough roto-molded and foam-filled pontoons, rod holders, a bimini and plenty of other options. They, frankly, look like a blast to rip around on, or cast from in inches of shallow water. Have a closer look with Senior Editor Chris Dixon.
Regulator 25 Walkthrough
November 7, 2024
Built on a deep-V platform and powered with twin 250-hp Yamahas, the Regulator 25 is ready to head offshore. Step aboard for a first look at the 2024 Fort Lauderdale Show.
Viking 74 Walkthrough
November 2, 2024
One of the stars of the 2024 Fort Lauderdale Show is undoubtedly Viking’s new 74, which replaces their popular 72. 60 years of dedication to building a better boat is certainly on display here.
Grady-White Freedom 345 Walkthrough
October 31, 2024
The latest dual console model from Grady-White offers big boat features along with fishability and entertaining space. Walkthrough the Grady-White Freedom 345 at her Ft Lauderdale debut.

Find a Used Yacht for Sale: Sailing Boats on the Market
Looking for a used yacht for sale? Here’s our pick of the latest sailing boats on the market.
Author
:
Sam Jefferson, Editor
Published Date:
10 February 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Yacht for sale - Rustler 42 - Cruachan  |  Photo credit: Red Ensign
Rustler 42 - Cruachan
Cruachan
is an iconic Rustler 42 from 2011 that has had very little use. She has been sailed in UK waters only and is in pristine condition. She has a blue hull and teak decks with oak interior, and two heads, and she’s had recent upgrades to standing rigging, plus electronics, sprayhood, electric tender outboard, and safety equipment. The package includes bowthruster, Webasto heating, hydrovane wind steering, Parasail and a cruising chute in snuffer. The broker tells us
Cruachan
is located in Falmouth on the hard ready for viewing, and offers a great opportunity to own one of the best bluewater yachts on the market.
Yacht for sale - Rustler 42 - Cruachan  |  Photo credit: Red Ensign
Year:
2011
LOA:
12.8m (42ft)
Beam:
4.06m (13.32ft)
Draft:
1.88m (6.17ft)
Displacement:
11,763kg
Hull Construction:
Glassfibre
Keel:
Long fin
Engine:
Nanni 50hp diesel inboard
Designer:
Stephen Jones
Accommodation:
7 berths in 3 cabins
Location:
Falmouth, UK
Contact:
red-ensign.com
Moody DS41 - Fox Hat
The Moody DS41 (Deck Saloon) designed by Bill Dixon has been exceptionally popular since its launch in 2020, and therefore the broker is pleased to be able to offer this fine example for sale in the UK.
Yacht for sale - Moody DS41 - Fox Hat  |  Photo credit: Michael Schmidt
Fox Hat
is the most popular 2 cabin and 2 heads layout version, and, within her top specification, has electric winches, bow and stern thruster, heating system, electric in-mast furling, upgraded sails, Elvstrom furling gennaker, microwave, dishwasher, and full
Raymarine
navigation suite. The interior of the award winning DS41 always feels bigger than her 41ft should allow and the benefits of the single level living concept on
Fox Hat
are immediately appreciated as you enter the saloon on exactly the same level as the cockpit. She has a stylish, light oak interior, twinned with a classic teak and holly striped floor with denim blue upholstery. Sleeping accommodation is made up of a spacious forward cabin with a large ensuite. The guest cabin is laid out with twin beds (which can be converted to a large double), and the heads (with shower) across the corridor, doubles up as a day heads. On a final note, the broker tells us that
Fox Hat
has only been privately used and available to purchase with VAT to be handled appropriately according to the purchaser and location of delivery.
Yacht for sale - Moody DS41 - Fox Hat  |  Photo credit: Michael Schmidt
Year:
2022
LOA:
12.52m (41.08’)
Beam:
4.20m (13.78’)
Draft:
2.14m (7.02’)
Displacement:
11,200kg
Hull Construction:
Glassfibre
Keel:
L-shaped
Engine:
Yanmar 4JH57, 57hp diesel
Designer:
Bill Dixon
Accommodation:
4 berths in 2 cabins
Location:
Hamble, Hampshire
Contact:
michaelschmidt.co.uk
Wauquiez Pilot Saloon 48 - Mahi Mahi
The Wauquiez Pilot Saloon 48 is designed for bluewater cruising and combines a powerful hull with a light-filled, raised saloon, offering panoramic views and a seamless connection between interior and exterior spaces.
Yacht for sale - Wauquiez Pilot Saloon 48 - Mahi Mahi  |  Photo credit: Grabu International
Mahi Mahi
represents a fine example of its high quality build and craftsmanship and offers the perfect balance between performance, comfort, and style.
Mahi Mahi
is hull number 14 and was delivered to her current owner in 2021 for light summer cruising in and around the Southern Baltic. She is a well-specified 3-cabin, 2-heads interior layout example and, according to the broker, is presented in great condition throughout. The broker also tells us that this yacht was originally commissioned with over 200,000 Euros spent in factory fitted options and therefore represents a significant saving over her new-build cost, with the added benefit of side-stepping the lengthy wait for a new delivery.
Yacht for sale - Wauquiez Pilot Saloon 48 - Mahi Mahi  |  Photo credit: Grabu International
Year:
2021
LOA:
14.95m (49’0")
Beam:
4.61m (15’0")
Draft:
2.1m (6’0")
Displacement:
14,000kg
Hull Construction:
Glassfibre
Keel:
Fin
Engine:
Yanmar 110hp diesel engine
Designer:
Berret-Racoupeau
Accommodation:
6 berths in 3 cabins
Location:
Thurø, Denmark
Contact:
grabauinternational.com

2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show: What to Look Forward To
The 2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show is on the horizon... Here’s what’s new!
Author
:
Milly Karsten, Digital Editor
Published Date:
19 December 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show  |  Photo credit: RYA
On 21st-22nd February 2026, Farnborough International is set to host the UK’s biggest celebration of dinghy sailing and watersports. Covering cruising, windsurfing, dinghy sailing, paddleboarding and winging, the show will bring the marine industry together and showcase the latest innovations across the sport - a brilliant way to kick off 2026!
What to Look Forward to at the 2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show
From new boats and cutting edge tech, to expert coaching sessions from leading names in the sport, this year’s show brings you a weekend of innovation and inspiration.
The 2026 programme is jam packed with exciting talks, demos, simulators and interactive displays and, this year, the show welcomes wingfoiler and presenter Rafferty Read to the hosting line-up.
RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show  |  Photo credit: RYA
The brand new feature we are looking forward to is the NextGen Zone, a designated space for young sailors and watersports lovers to get stuck into workshops and sessions designed to build the next generation’s confidence and skills.
“What makes the RYA Dinghy & Watersports Show so special is how it unites the whole community. From dinghy racers and windsurfers to paddleboarders and foilers, top names in our sport and the best marine brands – everyone’s ready and willing to share their knowledge to set you up for success,” said Show Manager Celia Edgington.
RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show  |  Photo credit: RYA
From trying the trapeze rig and wing, and experiencing sailing simulators or the on-the-water 360 virtual reality, to boat repair workshops and grabbing some brand new kit, the 2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show is one you won’t want to miss!
For the Family…
As in years before, the show is a perfect weekend activity for the whole family. Bringing young visitors together, the ever-popular Treasure Hunt invites little ones to keep on the lookout for their favourite marine wildlife, collecting clues and stickers throughout the show, with a chance to win some brilliant prizes.
RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show  |  Photo credit: RYA
Tickets
To book your ticket,
visit the 2026 RYA Dinghy and Watersports Show website
. Prices start from £15 (children under 15 go free with a ticketed adult).
RYA members
are able to access a free two-day ticket.
Photo credit: RYA

A Historic Sydney-Hobart: Photos and Race Report
This winter, on 26th December 2025, the crews of the Sydney-Hobart race set off and made history... Sam Jefferson reports.
Author
:
Sam Jefferson, Editor
Published Date:
3 February 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Sydney-Hobart start  |  Photo credit: Kurt Arrigo
The Sydney-Hobart Race remains one of the classics of the racing calendar, with the 2025 iteration, leaving Sydney on Boxing Day, delivering the first ever win for a female skipper. Jiang Lin, co skippering the JPK 10.30
Min River
with veteran racer Alex Loison, became the first woman to ever win the race. Meanwhile the pair also became the first double-handed crew to lay claim to the famous Tattersall Cup. Frenchman Loison could savour a remarkable hat trick in 2025, having also won the
Rolex Fastnet Race
and La Solitaire du Figaro.
As ever though, the initial focus was on line honours, with this year’s iteration serving up an intriguing clash of the super maxis. There were no fewer than six 100ft super Maxis taking part;
LawConnect, Master Lock Comanche
,
SHK Scallywag
,
Wild Thing 100, Palm Beach XI
and
Maritimo 100
. Many of these yachts had a long pedigree in the competition and, the competition for line honours in this edition went to the wire.
LawConnect - Sydney-Hobart  |  Photo credit: Andrea Francolini
All eyes were on the current course record holder
Master Lock Comanche
and
Law Connect
which have previously enjoyed epic tussles in the Tasman Strait while the return of nine times line honours winner
Palm Beach XI
(
ex Wild Oats
) added further spice to this clash of the titans.
The fleet set off into strong headwinds with a hefty swell which made for a gruelling thrash out of the Bass Strait.
Law Connect
led
Comanche
over the start line with
Palm Beach
just behind this pair. With boats dropping out regularly as the fleet thrashed its way to Tasmania,
Comanche
was able to carve out a lead of some 7.5nm as the fleet entered the final strait into Hobart. At this point, the race took a dramatic turn with the breeze shutting down completely leaving
Comanche
utterly becalmed while the chasing fleet of
LawConnect, SHK Scallywag 100
and
Lucky
closing in to the point where they were all within a few 100 metres of each other.
“There was just no wind — it totally dissipated,” James Mayo, co skipper of
Comanche
explained: “The boats behind caught us, brought the breeze back down, and then we all parked up for a while. But everyone stayed calm.
“We saw puffs developing near the shore, started moving, and suddenly we had a proper boat race. We were only a length apart for close to 100 miles. That was special.”
Ultimately,
Comanche
was able to re open her lead over her rivals and arrived in Hobart in a time of 2d 5h 3m 36s to take the win. An indication of how unfavourable conditions were is that this was nowhere near her record time of 1d 9h 15m. Bitter rival
LawConnect
came second followed by
SHK Scallywag
.
Min River, Sydney-Hobart Race  |  Photo credit: Kurt Arrigo
With line honours settled, attention turned to the
IRC
overall result and this was scooped by the double-handed JPK10.30
Min River
skippered by Chinese/Australian short-handed sailor Jiang Lin with Alexis Loison. The victory was hard fought and Lin and Loison were originally sitting in second place overall. However, following a protest by the Race Committee against the double-handed crew on
BNC - my::Net/LEON
(Michel Quintin/Yann Rigal) for a rule infringement, the latter was penalised one hour and five minutes by the jury. That result delivered the overall victory to
Min River
and history was made in the process.

How Has Dylan Fletcher Risen to the Top?
Dylan Fletcher has been on a journey since he put the Kiwis to the sword in the Tokyo Olympics. He is currently riding on the crest of a wave.
Author
:
Andy Rice
Published Date:
22 January 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
SailGP Dylan Fletcher  |  Photo credit: Ricardo Pinto for SailGP
Dylan Fletcher has been on quite the journey over the past two years. To steer
Emirates GBR
to victory in the Grand Final of this season’s Rolex SailGP Championship places him firmly in the top echelon of the world’s best professional sailors. Two years ago he was struggling to get noticed, and this coming after winning an Olympic gold medal and the International Moth World Championship.
For many years on the Olympic circuit, Fletcher was one of a cohort of sailors playing second fiddle to the unstoppable Kiwis Pete Burling and Blair Tuke in the 49er skiff class. While Fletcher pushed the New Zealanders hard, he never managed to beat them at a major championship - until it mattered the most.
Sailing with crew Stu Bithell, Fletcher went into the final race of the Tokyo Games needing to finish two places ahead of Burling and Tuke. It all came down to a joust between the Brits and the German crew of Erik Heil and Thomas Ploessel. Fletcher and Bithell just managed to tip their 49er over the front of a small wave and surf across the finish line by little more than a metre ahead of Germany and two places in front of the Kiwis to seize a shock gold medal. Fletcher and Bithell had revealed the invincible New Zealanders to be mortal after all.
Despite that shock defeat in Tokyo, Burling and Tuke have proven themselves to be the outstanding talents of their generation. Aside from their six 49er world titles and their three Olympic medals, together Burling and Tuke have helped propel Emirates Team New Zealand to three successive America’s Cup victories. They even came within a gnat’s whisker of winning the Volvo Ocean Race on separate boats in that incredible three-way battle to the finish before being pipped to the post in The Hague by the Chinese entry, Dongfeng Sailing Team.
But the title that yet eludes Burling and Tuke is season victory in SailGP. Considering the breeze wasn’t that strong on the Sunday of Abu Dhabi - only just enough to keep the boats foiling around the race track - it was a nailbiter of a good finale. The Australians, Kiwis and British all had a go at taking the lead before Fletcher and strategist Hannah Mills’ decisive move to take the right-hand mark at the windward end of the course. The British gybed into a stronger gust near our position in the waterside media centre, while their Australasian rivals ran out of gas on the far side of the course.
Fletcher has now got the better of Burling both in the Olympic finals and SailGP finals. His season in SailGP has been sensational, the British winning the most events, taking the highest number of season points and being the most consistent performers out of those hectic reaching starts.
With a big game of musical chairs going on in a very busy transfer market between the end of Season Five and the start of Season Six in January, Fletcher now finds himself reunited with his old mate Stu Bithell who moves from Germany to the British F50 to take up the wing-trimmer’s role left vacant by Iain Jensen (another 49er Olympic gold medallist) who steps into Tom Slingsby’s Australian boat after Chris Draper (a 49er Olympic bronze medallist and former World Champion) steps into the new Swedish Artemis F50 skippered by Nathan Outteridge (who won 49er Olympic gold with the aforementioned Jensen). Phew!!! And that’s not even the half of it.
It’s interesting to note the vital statistics of the four skippers that went into the final weekend in Abu Dhabi still with a shot at winning the season. Three of the four - Burling, Fletcher and Spain’s Diego Botin - have won Olympic gold in the 49er. The odd one out is Australia’s Tom Slingsby who is also an Olympic goldmedallist, but in the Laser. And three of the four - Slingsby, Burling and Fletcher - have won a Moth Worlds.
Fletcher is the up-and-coming force in pro sailing right now, so much so that it’s easy to overlook the fact that he’s 37 years old, in fact three years older than Burling who has already achieved so much yet still only 34. After banging on the door of Sir Ben Ainslie’s America’s Cup base, begging to get a chance to prove himself, Fletcher’s skill and calm under pressure was one of the revelations of the America’s Cup in Barcelona.
Not that he was a complete rookie coming into SailGP for the first time, as Fletcher was the driver for GBR in Season One of the start-up circuit back in 2019, but the boats and the standard of racing have changed significantly over the five years that he was absent from the competition.
Fletcher has roared back into SailGP, and has been cited by many pundits as the MVP (most valuable player) of Season Five. Now with Stu Bithell jumping on board, it will be fascinating to see if Emirates GBR can carry the momentum into 2026.

Offshore Rising Stars Ellie Driver & Ollie Hill: Young Short-Hand Ambition and Success
Rising stars of solo offshore sailing – Ellie Driver and Ollie Hill – are taking on some of short-handed racing’s toughest tests in the Figaro fleet. Georgie Corlett-Pitt tells their story.
Author
:
Georgie Corlett-Pitt
Published Date:
2 December 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Ellie Driver & Ollie Hill sailing in the Figaro  |  Photo credit: Vincent Olivaud
Just a few seasons ago, Ellie Driver burst onto the offshore scene racing two-up with her dad, Jim Driver, onboard their Sun Fast 3300,
Chilli Pepper
– a move that was, in equal measures, bold and inspiring. For sure, taking on some of the UK’s top offshore events two-up and while still in her teens marked a significant step-up for Ellie – her previous sailing had begun in Optimists at Trearddur Bay SC and taken her on to compete internationally with the 420 British Team. But when Covid forcibly halted that campaign, about the only way to keep racing was double-handed offshore, and while sailing with her dad may have seemed a little unusual, Ellie says, they have always got on well and “it made perfect sense at the time”.
Ellie Driver and Jim Driver
Now, 2025 has brought another big step for 23-year old Ellie. A new campaign in the foiling one-design Beneteau Figaro 3 has already seen her take on one of the toughest solo offshore races,
La Solitaire du Figaro Paprec
. Completing it was, she says, “a dream come true”, and a major tick towards her ultimate goal of the 2032 Vendée Globe.
When we speak, Ellie has just returned from competing in the Rolex Middle Sea Race – a 600nm sprint from Malta around Sicily – on board Gordon Ketelbey’s fully-crewed TP52
Zen
. While Ellie recounts the race’s most memorable moments – from the dramatic start line within Valletta’s Grand Harbour, to sailing past Stromboli as it spewed clouds of ash – the sailing itself she says made a complete contrast to the short-handed racing she is accustomed to, with “plenty of corners and opportunities to learn, as well as a lot less personal space and a lot more sleep”.
Short-Handed Sailing
More used to handling the boat entirely by herself and napping for mere minutes at a time, this season Ellie has clocked up some 7,300nm of short-handed sailing, including her first transat.
Ellie on her first  single-handed race,  the brutal La Solitaire  |  Photo credit: T Campion
Undoubtedly, short-handed sailing is where her passion lies. She attributes much of that to her dad, himself an accomplished offshore racer, who spent many years racing for the Army sailing team, and who Ellie describes as having “endless energy and drive”. She recalls childhood days sailing a Mirror with her dad, before progressing to squads. So when Jim suggested they give double-handed keelboat racing a try, Ellie jumped at the idea, though, she says, not without a little trepidation at racing bigger boats.
The twist came in the timing – and in Ellie’s eagerness to make the most of any opportunity.
In 2020 Ellie took up a place at Southampton University studying Maritime Engineering. Instead of languishing in her room during the pandemic lockdowns, she threw herself into RORC’s race scene – skeletal that year through necessity and comprising mostly short-handed events – and discovered a welcoming community embracing the burgeoning double-handed scene. At the time, the discipline was also under consideration for the
Olympics
.
Taking part in their first offshore double-handed race later that year, Ellie and her dad were pleased to find themselves ahead of Shirley Robertson and Jeremy Waitt on JPK1010
Jangada
. That was until a radio call from Shirley informed them they’d missed a mark and needed to double-back several miles to correct their mistake. It was a classic rookie error, but nonetheless Ellie appreciated the inclusive approach, saying: “It was a good environment to be in as it let you push yourself harder.”
A Tough Offshore Test
Perhaps the ultimate test for Ellie and Jim came in taking on RORC’s Sevenstar Round Britain and Ireland Race – an arduous 1,800nm circumnavigation that took them exactly two full weeks to complete. This time, they finished just ahead of Shirley who was sailing two-up with Dee Caffari. Impressively, Ellie became the youngest skipper to complete the race, and one of only five female sailors to ever do so double-handed. It was a real testament to her determination in overcoming any doubts that the wider sailing community may have cast over her age, gender or ability, and an achievement that earned her Sailor of the Year at STY&Y’s British Yachting Awards later that year.
It was about that time that Ellie had applied for
The Magenta Project’s mentoring scheme
. She explains how a conversation with her mentor, Annie O’Sullivan, a skipper with The Maiden Factor, had encouraged her to vocalise her Vendée Globe ambitions for the first time, and crucially, Annie’s positive response had given her the confidence to pursue it.
It made Ellie’s foray into the Figaro class the following year all the more important, and she was delighted to win at the EUROSAF Double-Handed Female European Offshore Championships with Catherine Hunt, and place second in the Mixed Double-Handed Worlds with George Kennedy. She also clocked up another Fastnet Race with her dad on
Chilli Pepper
, this time finishing 10th double-hander and second female skipper and second youth skipper overall.
By now, Ellie and Jim had sailed every event they could together, and the time came to graduate from their dad-daughter duo. She smiles as she acknowledges her own highly competitive nature, saying: “We learned a huge amount together and had a lot of fun, but felt like it was naturally time to move on.”
There was just one thing stopping her from her next step: “I had promised my parents I would graduate uni before becoming a pro sailor, so I decided I should probably do my exams rather than sail across the Atlantic!”
While Ellie paused her sailing to complete her Masters degree, in the meantime, dad Jim had been turning his attention to developing the RORC Griffin programme, encouraging young sailors into offshore racing and creating opportunities to hone skills and gain experience that will ultimately bolster the UK yacht racing scene. With hundreds of young people already benefitting over the first few years of the scheme, Ellie is full of praise for her dad’s involvement and the impact that it is having.
Ambitions Aligned: Ellie Driver & Ollie Hill
For Ellie, the moment she graduated she set her sights on her next challenge – the Transat Paprec; acquiring her own Figaro – named for her inspirational partnership with the support charity, Women’s Engineering Society – and teaming up with co-skipper, Ollie Hill for this unique double-handed/mixed-gender event.
Ollie and Ellie  |  Photo credit: Figaro
Ollie joins our chat also freshly returned from completing the Middle Sea Race on Simon Toms’ SunFast 3300,
Zephyr
– notably having won IRC6 and 10th overall on IRC. The affable 25-year old explains that, after graduating in Yacht Design from Southampton Solent, he was inspired to step into the Figaro fleet by Nigel King, Volvo Ocean Race sailor and coach to Shirley and Dee’s two-handed campaign, with whom he had worked on several pro sailing campaigns.
While Ollie’s ambitions stop short of his own Vendée Globe campaign, his focus is firmly on winning La Solitaire du Figaro, a goal that stands as an achievement in its own right. The class, he says, “offers very pure racing and creates incredibly talented sailors in a remarkably short time”, noting that not only does the fleet feed many top-level sailors into Class40 and IMOCA campaigns, but it retains a very high calibre of sailors, and as such, the class is notoriously difficult to dominate. He points to Alexis Loison’s win of this year’s La Solitaire on the 19th attempt, showing an inspiring combination of talent and tenacity. Ollie’s own extensive experience shows similar qualities: as a 17-year old, he set a world record for sailing around the Isle of Wight in 11.5 hours – in an Optimist! Later, after competing at international level with the British squads, he went on to race in IRC and SB20s in the Middle East, then taking on the
ARC
, and racing on the Caribbean circuit before a return to the UK for university, which launched him into the RORC racing scene at the front of Cape 31 and IRC fleets. Making his mark in short-handed sailing, he won the opening leg of the IRC Double-Handed Euros in 2024 with Josh Dawson.
After realising their ambitions were closely aligned, in early 2025, Ellie and Ollie relocated to
Brittany
, the epicentre of offshore racing. A brave decision for the young couple but one that gained them an invitation to join the Lorient Grand Large academy. Offering an intense programme for solo sailors focussing on nav, weather routing and endurance sailing skills, the academy provides a level of training that’s been noticeably absent in the UK since the demise of the Artemis Offshore Academy, nearly a decade ago. While schemes like the Griffin Project are now working to redress that skills gap on home waters, the move to France meant Ollie and Ellie could fast-track their learning.
Ellie Driver & Ollie Hill
There, they have found appeal in the focused and professional approach to campaigning, and were delighted by the ready acceptance they’ve been offered as serious contenders – despite the language barrier! – as Ollie describes: “Everyone is there to help each other. There’s a strong sense of camaraderie because everyone knows how tough this kind of sailing is.”
Ellie says the training has sharpened her self-taught approach and pushed her sailing to a higher standard.
Taking on the Figaro: A Learning Experience
The first chance for the duo to test their newly acquired Figaro skills came in April last year year, with the double-handed Transat Paprec – a 3,890nm race which took them 19 days, racing from Concarneau in Brittany to St Barths in the Caribbean, via a mark in the Canaries.
Their sense of achievement in completing such a marathon was overwhelming. Ellie describes it as an “adventure” in which they faced everything, from autopilot failure to winds of 60kts – but says that above all it was “a great eye-opener as to areas we could improve”.
Relief at the finish of the 3890nm Transat  |  Photo credit: Vincent Olivaud
Immediately after returning to Europe, they began preparing for the 1,850nm single-handed multi-stage race, La Solitaire du Figaro Paprec, from Rouen on the northern French coast, to Vigo, Spain and back to Normandy. Ollie – having only initially set out to do the double-handed Transat – decided to continue training, digging into savings to charter his own Figaro,
Connected by Water
.
Start day arrived, and having done only one solo race previously– over just two days and in light airs – Ellie and Ollie set off from Rouen, each determined to give La Solitaire their best shot; the two Brits among only five non-French sailors in the 35-strong fleet.
With three, five-day legs and as little as 24 hours onshore in between, both agree the experience was compelling yet exhausting: from dodging the tail end of an Atlantic storm and criss-crossing the Channel’s busy shipping lanes multiple times, to battling the wilds of Biscay then floundering in zero wind.
“With a fleet of 35 identical boats racing in such close quarters, Figaro racing is almost like dinghy racing – it’s impossible to relax,” explains Ollie, who says by leg three they were both feeling the effects of cumulative sleep deprivation. Although they had support from a préparateur on shore, as well as parents and friends handling logistics, the very short stopovers made for little respite. “You start to question your decisions,” says Ollie. “That was, mentally and physically, the most taxing leg. But it’s incredible what your body can do without sleep for so many days in horrendous circumstances, even if, at the time, you are running on adrenaline – and the stress of such a compressed fleet. However, by the end everyone completely crashed!”
Placing 19th overall and 5th Rookie Ollie was also the first non-French finisher and was duly awarded the coveted Vivi Trophy. It was an outstanding result, particularly given the short preparation period, but Ollie is quick to emphasise that it illustrates how the Figaro class, while open to newcomers, demands high standards of professionalism.
In at the Deep End
For Ellie, embarking on her first full-on solo race brought a sense of “in at the deep end”.
“It was great fun, but also a huge mental and physical challenge,” she says. “Pretty quickly, I realised the enormity of what I had taken on. The biggest challenge came in the last-minute change of course on the first leg due to the weather, which threw up a lot of unknowns. But once I had completed leg one, it gave me confidence to go on and complete the next two. The race was brutal, and looking back, I’m incredibly proud of what I’ve achieved.”
Ellie Driver and Ollie Hill - 2025 has been an incredible  season for these two  ambitious sailors  |  Photo credit: Vincent Olivaud
When I ask if they’d take on La Solitaire again, it’s a resounding “yes” from both.
While Ollie sums it up as a “hidden gem of a race – relentless, gruesome, but one that brings out brilliant sailors”, Ellie explains that their search for partners to enable them to continue their campaigns is already well underway. “It’s an ongoing battle,” she says. “Do you spend your time trying to get more partners, or do you go out training and improve your sailing? Realistically, both are as important as each other.”
It’s hard not to sense a little frustration, naturally, but nonetheless, there’s excitement there too, as they both acknowledge that success comes as much from putting in the hard yards on shore as it does from churning up the miles offshore. And if the competitive yet grounded determination they both have shown so far are anything to go by, then there’s no doubt these two will be ones to back when it comes to Britain’s future success in offshore racing.

How to Go Racing in the Caribbean: Chartering for All Skill Levels
Whether you’re a seasoned sailor or a curious novice, racing in the Caribbean is what dreams are made of, offering charter options for all skill levels writes Sue Pelling
Author
:
Sue Pelling
Published Date:
9 September 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
LV Yachting, RORC, Caribbean 600 race start  |  Photo credit: Alex Turnbull
Race charter in the Caribbean is not only all about sailing in turquoise waters with consistent trade winds and dramatic island backdrops but it’s also about being part of a lively, local tradition. You’ll find that many Caribbean islands that run regattas are major social and cultural events, blending sport, music, food, and island spirit. This cocktail of hard core, adrenaline-driven racing, and legendary rum punch-fuelled après-race parties are what Caribbean regattas are all about because above all they foster unforgettable memories and friendships that will probably last a lifetime.
The main Caribbean race season runs from January to late April, which not only avoids the hurricane season, but also gives overseas visitors plenty of time to make their trip south worthwhile, with many, particularly pro teams, using the Caribbean circuit as an ideal shake down to the forthcoming northern hemisphere race season.
Caribbean race charter  |  Photo credit: Gabe Rovick
There is a packed calendar of regattas to choose from and, thanks to the Caribbean Sailing Association (
CSA
), which promotes each event with its own identity, dates rarely clash, which means you could realistically take part in the whole Caribbean race series.
If however, holiday time is limited, you may want to return to your favourite haunt with events like
St Maarten Heineken Regatta
, the
BVI Spring Regatta & Sailing Festival
, or
Antigua Sailing Week
some of the most popular. For something different, yet equally fun and extremely competitive, maybe consider St Thomas International Regatta or St Barths Bucket.
Given its popularity, there’s a good choice of race yacht charter companies serving the Caribbean and covering events and feeder race/cruises such as the
World Cruising Club Atlantic Rally for Cruisers
(ARC and ARC+) an event which sets off across the Atlantic from Gran Canaria to the Caribbean mid to late November. This means there are many opportunities to sign up for a berth on either a private charter yacht, or charter yacht through companies such as LV Yachting, Go West Sailing, or Firstaway Yacht Charter.
While many companies offer crewed or bareboat charter options, a growing sector is individual berth charter. Sea to Sky Sailing primarily offers individual berth race charter opportunities aboard its race optimised Salona 45,
Panacea X
. Katy Campbell (Sea to Sky Sailing Director and lead race skipper) commented: “We are a frequent feature of the podium across the whole Caribbean circuit, and in 2025 were awarded the Traveler’s Trophy for being the top performing boat of the year. We are usually the only female-skippered boat on the circuit, often the only one flying the maple leaf, and one of the few associated with an RYA training centre.
Antigua English Harbour, Nelson's Dockyard  |  Photo credit: Shutterstock
“For the 2025/2026 Caribbean season, we will be racing in St Lucia’s Mango Bowl, Barbados Sailing Week, Rum to Spice Offshore Race, Grenada Sailing Week, RORC Caribbean 600, St Maarten Heineken Regatta, BVI Regatta and Antigua Sailing Week. We’re looking forward to another great season and look forward to having many familiar faces back on board as well as new crew joining us from all over the world.”
Another popular company is LV Yachting which specialises in race yacht charters across the Caribbean, and is passionate about connecting teams with high-performance yachts – from Volvo Ocean 65s and ORC winners to multihulls and top-tier racer cruisers. LV’s Chris Bruty commenting on what’s on offer said: “We offer whole-boat and crewed charters for events including the RORC Caribbean 600, Heineken Regatta, BVI Spring Regatta, Antigua Sailing Week and more. With 15+ years’ experience, we tailor every charter to the crew’s needs, handling logistics like berthing, race entry, and concierge support. Whether you’re a seasoned campaigner or new to Caribbean regattas, our personalised service ensures a competitive, seamless and unforgettable racing experience.”
As well as being one of the key cruising charter companies, Sunsail also offers a range of race charter options in the Caribbean. Caroline Barbour, Sunsail’s Marketing Manager UK/ZA/AU said: “Our team of expert holiday planners can offer advice on chartering a bareboat or skippered yacht, suitable for seasoned crews and those new to racing. The key events for next season include the BVI Spring Regatta, Antigua Sailing Week, Grenada Sailing Week, and the St Maarten Heineken Regatta.
“Customers benefit from the maintained charter fleet and access to Sunsail’s experienced skippers. It’s an excellent way to gain race entry, shore-side support, and seamless participation in top-tier Caribbean regattas. Off the water, racers enjoy legendary socials, island hospitality, and an unbeatable party atmosphere that comes with every regatta.”
Second Star Sailing, which participates in events such as Grenada Sailing Week, RORC 600 Caribbean, St Maarten Heineken Regatta, and St Barths Bucket also specialises in women’s sailing, and is particularly keen to promote its Women at the Helm in the Caribbean. Second Star Sailing’s Aliya Brown commenting on this popular programme said: “Through our all-women race team programme, Women at the Helm, Second Star Sailing is happy to support women in competitive sailing. Our two-week package is meant to give female sailors more confidence and skills.
“It includes a full week of race training skills to support passionate women in developing their skills in any role on a racing team, whether they are helm, trimmer, bow, or tactician. During the second week, we will be on the water for Antigua Sailing Week, racing with a crew that is excited to share their knowledge. Based in Antigua, we are dedicated to getting more women involved in racing by giving them access, mentoring, and world-class training in a lively regatta setting.”
Finally it’s worth remembering that access to the Caribbean with direct flights from the UK to many islands makes chartering a yacht a good option particularly if leisure time is more restricted. If you are flexible with time, you may be able to find some good last-minute deals, so it’s worth keeping an eye out online (see Useful contacts below).

Why Our Editor Would Chose the New Balance 580 For an Ocean Crossing
Balance catamarans specialises in producing innovative high performance bluewater catamarans. Its new 580 is an impressive addition to its portfolio.
Author
:
Sam Jefferson, Editor
Published Date:
19 February 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Balance 580  |  Photo credit: Balance Catamarans
Are you in the market for a cruising catamaran, that’s not only fast and comfortable, but also fit for an ocean crossing?
Our Editor Sam Jefferson takes the new Balance 580 out for a test, marking it one of the best
new sailing boats
on the market, and the boat he would chose to cross an ocean or long passages.
It’s at the more expensive side of the market but, as Sam discovered, you’ll no doubt feel the benefits of this top-end catamaran.
new Balance 580 catamaran  |  Photo credit: Balance Catamarans
Editor’s Review of the New Balance 580
Ever sailed on a Hobie cat? Even if it’s just a knockabout Hobie Wave you soon discover that these compact, uncomplicated dinghies provide some of the most exhilarating sailing imaginable. Founder Hobie Alter was a man who took multihull sailing from relative obscurity in the 1960s to becoming a worldwide phenomenon. Now, another question – ever sailed a Cruising catamaran? Well, if you were unfortunate to sail some of the early models then you’ll know that many of them (not all) seemed to owe almost nothing to the nimble Hobie cats. Switching from a Hobie to cruising catamaran used to be like going from your lightest most comfortable
deck shoes
and suddenly donning a pair of ill fitting lead lined boots. Sailing these boats was the opposite of life affirming and often the steering was so slow and vague that you could find yourself wandering off on strange parabolas as you desperately tried to re centre the wheel. It is not what sailing should be all about and a strange oxymoron that the catamaran could embody both the best and worst of sailing.
One man who knows more about that than most is Philip Berman; philosopher, sage and founder of Balance Catamarans. Berman was a sun drenched Californian kid straight out of an early Beach Boys album when he won the Hobie Cat World Championship back in 1979. That event was held in South Africa and, little did Berman know that destiny was giving him a hefty nudge and a wink when he won that event at the southern tip of the continent. Many years later in 2013 he would return to a country he had grown to love with the aim of producing a cruising catamaran that could provide at least some of the thrills that the Hobie had afforded him as a youth.
new Balance 580 catamaran  |  Photo credit: Balance Catamarans
The result was Balance Catamarans, a company that has gone from strength to strength ever since. Working on the basis that cruising catamarans should be fast, comfortable and fun, the company has built up an impressive range. Costs have been kept down thanks to the relatively cheap price of labour in South Africa, while the design team, driven on by Berman’s vision, has delivered a number of innovative features to their catamarans over the years, perhaps most notably the Verso Helm which tackles the classic problem faced by multihulls of where to put the helm without compromising visibility, a feeling of being enclosed and also being involved with the rest of the crew. The Verso Helm could be set in three different positions and heights, a superb way of dealing with a thorny issue.
The Balance 580 is the latest addition to the range and, at 58’ in length sits between the 540 and 750. This is a big catamaran with serious potential for speed, space and luxury. The ideal mile muncher if you were considering an extended bluewater cruise. Designed by Antoine du Toit and built in South Africa at Cape St Francis – where the reels of the iconic surf movie Endless Summer were shot. Anyway, that’s not important right now, but it does add a pleasing backdrop to proceedings.
The concept behind the 580 is unquestionably fast luxury cruising. To this end, the boat features daggerboards, a powerful rig and a relatively light hull. Construction can be done in full carbonfirbe but Balance generally advise against this due to the increased noise in a seaway that comes from a carbonfibre hull. Thus in standard form, the hull is glassfibre but the ancillaries and hull reinforcement are in carbonfibre. The test boat I sailed out of Cannes in France was mostly standard and the weight was 16,000kg. The boat had already made the voyage from South Africa all the way to the Med so, as you can imagine, she was well loaded up for cruising. The big non-standard item was in boom furling which gave the boat’s main a bit less roach compared to a standard 580. It’s worth noting that all Balance catamarans are semi custom so each will vary somewhat in terms of layout and setup. The fundamentals remain the same.
I was actually fortunate to sail past the Balance in the Bay of Cannes the day before with the wind blowing 20-25kts. The sight of this sleek, purposeful yacht slashing through the waters at double digit speeds was an impressive one. The boat looks stylish and purposeful on the water.
On Deck
You step aboard via broad aft platforms with a nice rail set inboard to ensure you don’t lose your balance – pardon the pun. The cockpit is enclosed by a latitudinal bench which has a BBQ and sink integrated into the port side. The tender is carried on davits aft of this and there is a solid solar pane set on top of the davits which also provides a bit of extra protection from the sun for your tender.
Forward of this to port is the dining area with a great day bed/lounging area above and to port which is very comfortable. The Bimini is solid and there is the option to have it in carbonfibre which keeps weight down in a strategic spot where the weight is carried high up. To starboard in the cockpit is a small seating area and the lower helm position with throttles. I have already touched on the Versa Helm but perhaps now is the moment to elaborate. This setup allows the wheel to pivot up or down so that you can steer from the cockpit or from a more conventional raised helm station to starboard of the cockpit. From the lower helm station you have decent visibility all round, thanks to the large windows in the saloon. There is also a clever instrument panel with throttle controls that slides in and out meaning it is out of the way if you are not helming from the lower steering position. The helmsman’s seat also stows away when not in use.
From here, there are then steps up to the upper helm position. On many catamarans, this helm position can be a bit isolated from the cockpit but I did not find this to be the case on the 580. In fact we did not use the lower helm position but I can see that if the weather was really wild, it would be nice to have the extra protection afforded by being fully in the cockpit.
new Balance 580 catamaran  |  Photo credit: Balance Catamarans
Step onto the side decks and there is plenty of space and good handholds going forward. The daggerboards are raised and lowered on jammers led aft, outboard of the cockpit which was simple enough. At the bow you have a long trampoline which actually runs right forward outboard to the sprit which gives a nice feeling of security. Aside from that, the anchor locker is set just in front of the coachroof and there are a couple of nice seats set into the pushpits on the sponsons.
Step into the saloon via sliding patio doors and you find yourself in a very bright space with a huge kitchen island which has storage inside and a TV which retracts into it. To port is a long galley section with sink and cooker. Storage is ludicrous and the kitchen island provides a nice bracing point making for a superb place to cook if you are on a long ocean passage. To starboard is the dining table with a huge raised lounging space aft of this from whence you can watch a film on that huge TV and also happily chat to the helmsman if he/she is helming from the lower helm station. Forward are twin banks of fridge freezers with a chart table. This is a stylish, light area which is beautifully connected.
Both hulls mirrored each other on this option although there is the choice of a more extensive owner’s suite. However, in standard layout, you have a decent sized double aft with lovely views astern then a lot of cupboard storage to port and a bathroom with separate shower to starboard. There is the option of a ‘Jack and Jill’ set-up with a shared bathroom and heads to either side. Forward is a double bed raised up and set longitudinally with light from the skylight and a portlight making for a nice space. Some may struggle with the height of the berth which is over 1m high although the steps up are well placed and the berth has a lovely feel. In the bow is a lovely heads with a totally separate shower cubicle forward. Forward of this is watertight access to a large sail locker. As mentioned, the layout in both hulls was identical on the test boat but should you go for an owner’s saloon option, then the whole port hull is devoted to an owner’s cabin with the bed set aft and a massive bathroom forward.
new Balance 580 catamaran interior  |  Photo credit: Balance Catamarans
Under Sail
The rig set-up featured three headsails including a Code 0, all on electric furlers. The Inner jib is on a self-tacker which keeps things simple. The boom is set up on bridle system at the back of the coachroof with no traveller. The whole of the coachroof is a clear, flat area with plenty of space for solar panels plus good access to the mainsail for stowing it away. It’s also at a decent height for handling and this is important as there is nothing worse than battling with these huge multihull mains while on your tiptoes. In terms of handling the boat, all controls are led back to the cockpit and there are electric buttons that can be operated with your feet for the winches, while the furlers have switches. All is clear and well labelled. There are four large winches within easy reach of the helm meaning this is a boat that could easily be singlehanded for everything except mooring. The only reason you’d need to leave the helm station is to raise or lower the daggerboards.
The boat test took place just off Cannes in the Cote d’Azur on a beautiful day that gave us a decent amount of breeze (about 10- 15kts) and very modest chop. The in-boom furling system behaved itself very well and soon we were enjoying push button sailing as we furled and unfurled the various foresails. The boat was very lively for a big cat and, although perhaps not quite in the league of a Hobie 16, certainly felt alive and responsive. The helm had excellent feel and she was a pleasure to handle. In the stronger gusts I got a hint at the immense power and speed potential that this boat has. We hit 12kts maximum with the Code 0 up about 60 degrees off the wind. On the wind we were doing 8- 9kts at times 35 degrees off the wind. This will be one of the heavier 580s around as it features an alloy rig and glassfibre coachroof and, as already mentioned, she was well loaded up with gear. Despite this, the boat felt lively while also suggesting that it would make short work of a long ocean passage.
Sam’s Verdict on the Balance 580
This is a superb bluewater cruiser that hits the balance between raw performance and comfort very well indeed. The boat didn’t feel like some amped up wild sportsboat but it was impressively quick. The quality of the fit out was also superb and the attention to detail was impressive with Balance’s desire to innovate, clear in many little touches throughout the boat. These boats are not cheap but the rewards are great and, when it comes to crossing an ocean, I’d be very much in favour of doing it on one of these.
Blue water 5/5
Performance 5/5
Looks 4/5
The Spec
LOA:
58’3” (17.78m)
LWL:
58’3” (17.78m)
Sail Area:
2,255.04 sq ft (209.5 sq m)
Beam:
28’2” (8.60m)
Draft:
Max: 10’99” (3.35m) Min: 5’4” (1.65m)
Displacement:
36,746.65lb (16,668kg)
Bridgedeck Clearance:
3’61” 1.1m
Engines:
Yanmar 4JH57 x SD60
2 x 43 kW / 2 x 57 HP
VersaDrive option:
2 x 15Kw / 20HP Integrel E-Drives
Battery Capacity
51.2kW 48 Volt Lithium Ion
Designers:
Phillip Berman and Anton du Toit
Price:
£2.1m (sail away) c£2.5m (as tested)
Contact:
balancecatamarans.com

Raising Children on a Boat: How a Life Under Sail Shapes Our Kids
Jess says that returning to the UK on holidays highlights the contrasting ways in which her children have been raised compared with their land-dwelling counterparts.
Author
:
Jess Lloyd-Mostyn
Published Date:
12 December 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Raising Children on a Boat  |  Photo credit: Shutterstock
My kids are confused. Whenever we have a holiday away from the boat, visiting friends and family in the UK, it becomes very apparent to us that they have been raised quite differently from their land-dwelling counterparts. They simply have a singular frame of reference for things, a more diverse spectrum of experiences and what they consider to be ‘normal’ can definitely result in a raised eyebrow or two.
Toasters, for example, flummox them. Sounds odd I know. They love toast and have a thing for sourdough bread. However, making toast onboard our sailboat involves using the grill of our oven, something that they know how to operate. So, when confronted with a shiny silver device atop a kitchen counter with unfamiliar buttons, dials and a lever, they turn to us in utter confusion as to how it works. And any nearby adults snigger or mutter comments under their breath about ‘common sense’.
Likewise, when it comes to other eating habits, our
children
understandably have been subjected to a multitude of practices. So, whereas at my parents’ house the norm might be tablecloths and napkins, side plates, dinner plates and an assortment of knives and forks for them to decipher, they are also very comfortable to dive into eating with their hands and roti breads, using a banana leaf plate when we share a meal with friends in Kuala Lumpur.
Similarly, their food preferences often tend toward the exotic. If one of our well-meaning aunts asks if they’d like a little snack they will most likely request some rambutan or pomelo, chicken satay or dumplings, tostadas or ceviche as opposed to a more predictable order for a cheese and ham sandwich. They are comfortable haggling for fresh produce in markets, something that is very ordinary in most of South East Asia. Understandably, they are surprised that the price of millionaire shortbread in our local branch of the Co-op is a fixed thing, non-negotiable.
Raising Children on a Boat  |  Photo credit: Holly Astle
And then, when we’re walking down the street, there is a whole other world of what is considered ‘normal’ to navigate. Saying “good morning” and “hi” on the streets of London is, sadly, very rarely done these days, and their overtures of polite friendship mostly go ignored. But saying “selamat pagi” in Malaysia or “Buenas noches” on the streets of
Mexico
is an everyday routine courtesy, especially from young children. It’s perhaps expected that they consider the general public in the UK to be somewhat aloof given the parallels they have felt.
I’m often on the alert for how streetwise they can be. When crossing the street, which way to look? I always encourage them to look both ways on both sides as they’ve lived in so many different driving systems, although donkeys and monkeys are both less likely on the roads here. They know that in Bali there are taxis and fake taxis, which are almost identical in their signage and graphics; in Thailand we all pile into ‘tuktuks’, but in eastern Indonesia we ride in crowded “bemo’ buses with music blaring, whilst in central America we travel on old US school buses, covered in graffiti murals, and repurposed as “collectivos”.
They also have a wider variety of milestone reference points that punctuate their year. Our family always observe Christmas and birthdays but the only tradition we hold to is to embrace it being different each time, in each part of the world. They know of mothers’ day being celebrated across a whole host of different months and in different ways. Other celebrations that we’ve incorporated into our calendar are things like Chinese New Year, Día de Muertos, and Independence days or National days depending on where we are.
I suppose it’s important to acknowledge that they are what is known as “third culture kids”. The three of them are British, with British parents, born on three different continents. And part of our sailing journey has been their education in many varied and rich, contrasting cultures, observing a number of different ways of living and values. Add to that the rules, beliefs and principles of living on the ocean, of being part of a team, and it’s really no surprise that they’re kind of unique.
Yes, it’s a childhood where they’ve had the importance of flexibility, empathy and autonomy demonstrated to them and highlighted as much as possible. So the use of a toaster or the questioning of the price at a supermarket doesn’t matter to me nearly so much as them having a sense of who they are both as individuals and as a crew. And some of it rubs off the other way too, as I smile and say “hello” in the street all the time now – raised eyebrows be damned.

Will the America’s Cup Survive?
No Americans in the America’s Cup? What does it all mean? Plus why the America’s Cup needs to up its game to survive.
Author
:
Andy Rice
Published Date:
4 December 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
America's Cup  |  Photo credit: Ian Roman / America's Cup
How can you have an America’s Cup if the Americans aren’t taking part?” It’s a question that I’ve been asked a couple of times by my non-sailing friends. Of course, you and I know – being the clever sailing intelligentsia that we are ;-) – that ‘America’ has nothing to do with the country. It’s the name of the yacht, silly!
Even so, for American Magic to pull out of the 38th edition of the America’s Cup means it will be the first time there has been no USA team participating in the event’s 175-year history. How has it come to this? Three months ago in this column I gave a cautious thumbs-up to Grant Dalton and Sir Ben Ainslie’s ability to put aside their differences and get some agreement across the line after 10 months of stalemate. All the challengers were said to be on board with the new democratic structure of running the Cup.
Now I’m feeling a bit stupid for having applauded the entente cordiale. Because suddenly things don’t seem so cordiale anymore. The entry deadline for challengers was 31 October and, as I write in mid-November, there is no list of confirmed entries published on the America’s Cup website. Presumably no news is bad news.
The requirement for teams to use their same hulls and masts from the 2024 Cup might be one reason why American Magic have decided not to go forwards for Naples 2027. Even though foil design and engineering is probably the most important factor in making an AC75 fast across a range of wind and wave conditions, the whole package clearly still matters. The Americans were a couple of clicks off the pace in Barcelona, so the idea of using the same base equipment from 2024 was probably not so appealing.
Some were hoping that Alinghi might make a comeback despite the Swiss team having already announced its disbandment back in April. With their boat suffering a catastrophic failure in a training session soon after they had been eliminated from the challenger series last time – not to mention breaking both of their masts – Alinghi would have felt that they were already starting the next campaign from a position of weakness.
The 37th Cup in Barcelona was a pretty good event and, if the Defender and Challenger of Record had been able to have an agreement already in place for the 38th Cup, then Dalton and Ainslie might have been able to realise their shared ambition to make the Cup commercially viable. But more than a year later and all the momentum from Barcelona has drained away. Naples must be starting to wonder what it has signed up to. Can we expect more than the
Kiwis
and Brits to show up? Well,
Luna Rossa
, obviously. But after that, the French? Who else?
Much as Dalton and Ainslie love the AC75, is it a flying boat too far, even for the America’s Cup? Not necessarily, if like Dalton you argue that the America’s Cup is a technology race. In one sense he’s right of course, because the AC75 is the most extraordinary feat of design and engineering that we’ve seen in the event’s 174 years. Most of us still marvel at how the thing works at all, let alone achieves speeds in excess of 55kts.
But the Cup is so many other things to other people too. Newport 1983, Fremantle 1987 and Valencia 2007 are widely heralded as the high watermarks of the modern era, because the challenger series was so vibrant. Eleven challengers from nine countries in Valencia; that felt like a global event with a bright future, possibly even a commercially viable one.
Commercial viability is now a distant prospect, a pipe dream. I hope Naples 2027 goes ahead on schedule and that it will be the last hurrah for the spectacularly space-age AC75. After that can we please go back to something more affordable, with wind (not batteries) as the only power source, with a large crew of sailors (yes, sailors, not athletes), and with sails that go up and down the mast so that we can tell whether the boats are going upwind or downwind? The whole foiling thing, and the commercial thing, is being executed much, much better by SailGP.
RORC keelboat racing  |  Photo credit: James Tomlinson
The big winner from the current unaffordability of the America’s Cup is the newly revived
Admiral’s Cup
. With the Cup having become a billionaires-only playground, the Admiral’s Cup has swept into the vacuum at the top of the sport and celebrated its return with 15 international teams competing last summer.
The momentum for high-level keelboat racing continues with the announcement of the Sardinia Cup’s revival. The last time this event took place was in 2012, and now it’s set to take place in June 2026. As with the Admiral’s Cup, teams representing their yacht clubs will take part with two boats each, with nationality being determined by the location of the club.
In my interviews with Sir Ben over the past months he has said that the Cup needs to achieve commercial viability or it will die. I have respectfully disagreed with him. The Cup will never die because it only requires one fool to take on the Defender. The worst fate that can befall the Cup is that it fades into irrelevance. Forget about the commercial dream, it’s nothing more than a shimmering mirage on the horizon. If only the current custodians of the Cup were prepared to take a step back from their obsession with the AC75, the recent success of the Admiral’s Cup points to a much healthier and economically sustainable future for the America’s Cup.
