A Challenging Transatlantic: ARC+ British Crews Share Their Tales on Arrival
We hear from some of the British cruisers who, having crossed the Atlantic with ARC+, have some stories to tell of the challenges they faced.
Author
:
Ali Wood
Published Date:
13 January 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Start of ARC+  |  Photo credit: Ali Wood
There’s a party atmosphere on the pontoons in Grenada’s Port Louis marina; horns honking, crews cheering as the last few boats of the
ARC+
rally arrive, having completed the second leg of their transatlantic.
The 2,200NM passage from Cape Verde is traditionally the more challenging of the two, and this year more so than most, with the later arrivals, such as Rustler 36
Restless of Stornaway
, tackling 30-knot winds and confused seas.
“It was hellish, absolutely hellish,” says skipper Ann MacNeil, but with a smile on her face as she’s handed a rum punch. Sailing with partner Tim Wright, she describes how they navigated into a wind hole, came out into a squally 48-knots and sailed for a week in a Force 8. “We’re not really racers at heart. We must have been the only yacht trying to slow ourselves down! You don’t get enough sleep when the weather’s a bit grim. You need to extend the pole, shorten the pole, roll the sail away. It’s mad.”
High winds  |  Photo credit: Vicky Ellis
Whilst the challenge for
Restless
, the smallest boat in the fleet, was the conditions, for fellow British cruisers
Watergaw
and
Canopus
, the ARC+ rally was testing because of breakages – the watermaker in one boat and the rig in the other. When things go wrong at sea, the skipper has to take charge. If the crew sees things differently; whether husband or wife, or parent and child, it can make or break the sailing experience. So when Sailing Today had the opportunity to meet crews at the finish line, it was fascinating to hear what they had to say …
Big Bang Theory
Laurie Ellis was on watch when the shroud snapped onboard
Canopus
, a Moody 44 skippered by his wife Vicky, a professional round-the-world sailor.
“I was down below when I heard a big bang, no warning. Initially I thought we’d hit something because the hull shook, but my brain quickly worked out we hadn’t because it didn’t sound like a fibreglass impact; it was a metal bang,” he says.
Whilst Vicky and their two children Jasper (9) and Saskia (5) slept, Laurie rushed on deck and saw that a broken shroud was dragging in the water, which also explained the tapping noise against the hull.
When he woke Vicky to explain the situation, her first reaction was doubt, but when she saw Laurie grappling with a huge mass of wire, she soon realised it was serious. Wearing head torches, they inspected the damage and saw that the casualty was the intermediate shroud; the one that runs from the mast at the upper spreaders to the lower spreaders then down to the deck chainplate. It had sheared at the top but it was difficult to see the full extent of damage at night.
A former professional racer, Vicky was familiar with losing stays and shrouds and got to work stabilising the mast with spare haylards and the running backstays which were usually used to counter the removable inner forestay.
“We nearly lost a stay in the
Clipper Race
,” she explains. “We had articulation issues with the forestay so we pulled into Hong Kong and Sir Robin came out and under his supervision, we lashed the problem area with Dyneema. The repair turned out to be bombproof.”
Vicky was confident she could solve the issue, but says it was interesting seeing it through Laurie’s eyes. “I remembered the first time I was in that situation and you don’t know how certain it is that it’s going to be ok. You just try to put on a brave face.”
“We’re both engineers,” adds Laurie. “Though Vicky is very much the sailor, I’ve done a lot of boat work in my time. We can sometimes have a very different outlook on situations.”
Fortunately, they’re a couple who can discuss these things calmly. “We’ve known each other a very long time, and are used to being in close proximity. We solved the problem through teamwork,” says Laurie.
VHF Proficiency
It also helped that the children were so calm and thought it all a bit of an adventure. Vicky and Laurie had previously discussed the liferaft drill with them, and they knew how to use the
VHF
. On leaving Gran Canaria, Vicky had even started a kids radio net to ensure that the 48 children on the rally – the highest number to date – had the chance to learn how to report their position.
“It’s important they know where the GPS coordinates are, and the COG and SOG,” she says. “It also gives them a chance to learn VHF skills such as finding the right channel and when to hold and let go of the button when they speak. And it was great fun too!”
It was 1am when the first shroud snapped, but when dawn came, the mast still had a lot of lateral movement with each wave. Vicky and Laurie realised that despite their efforts, it was likely that the other side’s intermediate shroud was going to fail, which it did at 3pm that afternoon.
“Thank god we had running backstays, and thank god Vicky very quickly stabilised the mast,” says Laurie. “That initial interaction between us was pretty quiet and we got things sorted. I feel really lucky to be with a skipper that has so much experience and that we’ve been together long enough to operate efficiently in these stressful conditions.”
Following sea - ARC+  |  Photo credit: Vicky Ellis
One Mile at a Time
It was actually the remainder of the voyage that Laurie found hard. Having stabilised the mast, prepped the children and taken additional fuel from another ARC+ boat, there was nothing left to do but motor conservatively for five days to the finish line.
“You’re trying to keep the boat moving and put on a brave face for the children. Actually, the intensity is quite high,” he says. “Vicky is much more accustomed to boat noises; the creaks, the rattles, the vibrations, all that visceral stuff you get from a boat. I found it much harder going.”
What made a huge difference for Laurie in making him feel less vulnerable, was having very clear conversations about where the risks lay and what mitigations they could take.
“If we were worried about it, we would create a plan around it – let’s monitor the movement of the mast, let’s make sure that we are both holding ourselves to account with rig checks. We were fully reliant on a very old engine, so we kept on top of that too. We’re engineers and that’s what we do. I think we managed that really well.”
Thanks to the Hydrovane self-steering, which ‘just went on relentlessly’ and the ‘fantastic’ Perkins M60 engine, which didn’t miss a beat, there were no further issues, but Vicky and Laurie were extremely cautious.
“We were in a delicate state because there was still panting on the rig. We couldn’t rest on our laurels,” says Vicky. “We just took each mile as it came. We deliberately didn’t post an ETA, just distance to run.”
Laurie adds: “That takes quite a lot of discipline. It’s really easy to assume, when you’ve 50 miles to go, that you’re nearly there, but you’re not. What if the engine packs in?”
When the friendly team at Port Louis Marina finally attached
Canopus
’s lines, Laurie felt the weight lift off his shoulders.
“It was the first point that we could actually say we’re here,” he says. “We worked well as a team, played to one another’s strengths and never let the situation get on top of us. We didn’t panic, instead we focussed on what the issue was and how to stabilise the situation. Maintaining normality for everyone onboard was key – the kids  even put on a nativity play! One last note – always carry more Dyneema than you think you could possibly need!”
Watergaw Woes
Whilst
Canopus
’s rig drama was unfolding,
Watergaw
’s crew were experiencing breakages of their own, which Eileen Anderson describes as ‘an advent calendar of problems!’ It seemed, as Christmas drew closer, each day had a new tribulation in store for the Southerly 135.
Angus and Alastair on Watergaw  |  Photo credit: Alastair Todd
Eileen and husband Alastair Todd, retired radiologists from Inverness, were sailing with their grown-up son Angus, one of six children. Experienced sailors, they’d already cut their teeth on a circumnavigation of Britain, having arrived in Southampton, fortuitously, on the birth of their first grandson. It seemed that luck was with them on their first sailing adventure but not this one.
The first ‘calendar door’ opened with a failed generator on the leg from Las Palmas to Cape Verde. Thanks to their solar panels, they managed power reasonably well during the day, but with big consumers onboard such as a fridge freezer, chartplotter, autopilot and microwave they found that once the sun went down the batteries struggled to keep up.
Fortunately Alastair diagnosed the fault as fuel starvation, something he was familiar with, having dealt with diesel bug on their round-Britain. Back then, they’d used Fuel Set to keep the bug in suspension, and cleaned the tanks out afterwards, noting the tarry deposit left all over the filters and bowl. They then sailed from Scotland to the Canaries without any problems, but once in the middle of the Atlantic, the big rollers really churned up the tank.
“This kind of thing often arises in a swell. When you’re getting thrown around and the fuel tank is half-empty, there’s a lot of sloshing and the detritus from the diesel bug gets mixed in,” explains Alastair. “The problem with biodiesel is that it contains ethanol, and this attracts water. As diesel bug grows on the interface of fuel and water, if there’s any condensation in the cold tank walls it grows there and drops down.”
Alastair with the generator on Watergaw  |  Photo credit: Ali Wood
After changing the filters, the diesel generator ‘hiccuped a bit’ but then with a ‘big whack of Grotomar’ added to the fuel in Cape Verde it ran smoothly.
Whilst Alastair was happy that the generator was working, Eileen felt it was a wakeup call. What if it broke down again and they didn’t have enough power for the watermaker? Should they get some more water before setting sail? By now, they were down to just one of the two tanks, but Alastair assured her the generator was fine, and besides, they could run the watermaker off the engine battery if needs be, desalinating 17 gallons of seawater an hour.
Plastic Debate
“We had a huge row about it because I wasn’t happy,” recalls Eileen. “We’d been told not to drink the water in Cape Verde, so I was all for filling the tanks with bottled water. Alastair and Angus didn’t agree; they didn’t want to leave all that plastic waste for the island to deal with. They said if we don’t need it, let’s not do it. They persuaded me I was worrying unnecessarily.”
Unfortunately, 18 hours after leaving Cape Verde, the watermaker broke. There was a leak and it shut itself down.
“It was one of the few times I really wish I hadn’t been right,” says Eileen. “It was a really low point for me.”
Alastair was sanguine. They weren’t sinking, but he didn’t want to make the situation any worse by trying to fix it and contaminating the freshwater tank with seawater. With any luck, an almost full tank of 300 litres should last two weeks between the three of them if they were careful.
“It was strict rationing,” says Eileen. “We washed up in seawater and cooked potatoes and pasta in half and half; there was no need to add salt to anything. The pasta was a bit firm, even though it was a 3-minute cook.”
Alastair fitted a galley tap, which ran off the watermaker skinfitting, allowing them to easily access seawater for washing up. However, the downside was that the pots and pans turned rusty! For showers they used buckets as well as a hose poked through the heads window, which ran from a solar-heated bag on the deck.
“To cap it all, with the water situation, we all went down with awful diarrhoea!” says Eileen. “That really spoiled it for me. I didn’t wash my hair until Day 14. My scalp itched and I got sores from washing in seawater. Towards the end we decided to use the three 25-litre containers we’d filled in the Canaries, and added chlorine tablets to them. But you worry that if something goes wrong with the boat you could be out for longer than planned.”
The kind of plastic wastage Alastair was trying to avoid. Credit: Alastair Todd
AIS Glitch
Next to go wrong was the AIS. Interestingly they could see other boats, but weren’t transmitting their own position; something they were made aware of by other ARC+ yachts in the vicinity.
“A bit more vigilance was required,” says Alaistair. “It made us more likely to switch on the radar and have a look around. Ninety nine percent of the time you’ll never see anything, but every now and then a containership will come by.”
The VHF failed too, and Alastair realised the two problems must be related because the AIS and VHF shared an aerial.
“We were ok because we had a handheld VHF and could still see on AIS and communicate to vessels if they came close. Plus we had radar,” says Alastair. “It wasn’t ideal but when we heard another boat had very real problems, it put it all in perspective.”
It turns out the other boat was
Canopus
, who had just lost their shrouds. Alastair and Eileen offered them fuel, but they’d already agreed to take some from
Fortuitous
, which was closer. They offered to take the children, but Vicky and Laurie felt they were safer as a family unit and it would be too dangerous to put them in the water.
“They were just amazing, the way they dealt with it” says Eileen. “Vicky maintained such a sense of humor throughout, and despite what she was going through, took the time to reassure me about the watermaker. She’d had the same situation in the Clipper with 22 of them, and they had to live off 750mm a day. I felt better after that!”
Fortunately Starlink was one of the bits of kit that worked brilliantly, allowing
Canopus
,
Watergaw
and
Fortuitous
to maintain contact with the rest of the fleet and rally organisers.
Alastair continued to mull over the VHF and AIS issues, and on day 15, he finally discovered the fault. He stripped back the headlining and used electrical contact grease on the connections. He then split the AIS and VHF and remade the link from the VHF to the mastfoot. The AIS signal light turned from red to orange to green. Success!
Son Angus meanwhile, was having ‘a whale of a time’. When he started the voyage, he didn’t know how to do a round turn and two half-hitches, but by now, he was relishing helming.
“The rougher the better,” laughs Eileen. “He’s the young blood with the ‘go faster’ mentality. He just loved it, especially the night shifts because he could trim the sails how he liked.”
What was particularly interesting for Eileen was watching ‘the experience versus the inexperience’. Whilst Angus wanted to try everything to make the boat go faster, Alastair was conservative, pointing out that the last thing they needed was more breakages.
“I just kept quiet,” says Eileen.
Night Sailing
Night passages in particular gave the trio their personal space, and a chance to be at one with the ocean. Whilst Alastair enjoyed the night skies, and identifying planets, Eileen took it as an opportunity to reflect on the loss of a friend, whose funeral she’d been unable to attend.
“That’s the downside of having too much time to think. At home, you put it in a box and get on with things but here it preys on your mind. But maybe it’s a good thing you have time to remember?”
Though Alastair was disappointed with the breakages, he feels that it could have been worse. “We didn’t suffer rig failure like Canopus, or water ingress or fire. You’ve got to be grateful.”
Eileen adds, “I think the shared experience brings you closer as a family. It was nice to reacquaint ourselves with our 20-something son. To see how his character’s developed and see the best of him.”
So what now for the family, having arrived in Grenada?
“Well, put it this way, we’re not selling the boat and flying home, so that’s a success” says Alastair. “We’re staying here for Christmas, returning to the UK for the birth of another grandchild, and then after that, will come back and sail the US coast, Panama Canal, the Azores and home.
So whilst this year’s ARC+ rally wasn’t the easiest transatlantic for Watergaw, Canopus and many other boats, clearly it hasn’t dampened their appetite for ocean sailing. Adversity, even if experienced differently by different crewmembers, can strengthen the bond between family members, and make them better prepared for their next adventure.
Watergaw crew  |  Photo credit: Arthur Daniels
The ARC+
The ARC+ rally, sister event to the Atlantic Rally for Cruisers, sets sail from Las Palmas, Gran Canaria in November, finishing in Port Louis Marina, Grenada. The rally is split into two legs. The first, to Cape Verde is 850NM (a passage of 5-7days), and the second is 2,150NM (12-16 days).
On arrival in Grenada, trips can be booked to explore the island’s many delights, including rum distilleries, chocolate factories, tropical gardens, waterfalls, forest trails and snorkelling in the world’s first underwater sculpture park. Find out more at
puregrenada.com

The State of the Industry: Insights from VentureSail
We caught up with VentureSail Director, Jo Downie, on the challenges of the Charter sector and how the industry is doing.
Author
:
Sailing Today
Published Date:
18 September 2025
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Jo Downie (left)  |  Photo credit: VentureSail
Tell us a bit about VentureSail. What does the company do, and how did it get started?
VentureSail
offers a variety of fully crewed sailing experiences across a fleet of unique and interesting vessels, run by passionate seafarers worldwide. Most of our boats are owner-operated, providing guests with access to first-hand knowledge of a location and the opportunity to learn from very experienced crews. Guests can join in without ever having set foot on a boat before.
The agency was started in 2018 after working with Nikki Alford, skipper and owner of
historic ship Bessie Ellen
. We identified a need to support vessels and operators like hers at sea, who struggle to respond to emails or navigate modern-day marketing for their bookings. So we created a bespoke agency that represented this unique type of charter holiday. We built the website and booking engine from scratch, enabling us to tailor our processes and marketing to each boat’s unique needs.
What is your role within VentureSail, and how did you get started in the role? Have you always been into sailing?
I’m Cornish, so the sea has always played a big part in my life, but I came to sailing later in life when water skiing was no longer good for me. I’m the managing director and founder of the company with a strong background in marketing and tech. I closed my marketing agency during COVID to focus on this business full-time, with the goal of expanding the charter holiday sector. Although this is a fairly niche business, we have now expanded into European markets and work with operators from all over the world. We are still based in Cornwall, with an all-female team, but have a global vision, we sail with all our boats and have a great work-life balance by the sea.
What are the challenges and rewards of working in this sector of the marine industry?
Of course, when your business is determined by weather, there are naturally challenges, with fast-paced decisions that have to be made to ensure guests have the best experience. The rewards are immeasurable, though. A particular reward for us is having so many first-time customers who get completely hooked on sailing and have continued on to cross the Atlantic. We are also proud that so many experienced sailors choose to join us around the world. Whether it’s guests who have previously owned their own boat, but now want the headaches of route planning and paperwork taken care of, or guests who did their day skipper 25 years ago, once onboard everyone’s skill level comes together to work as a team - an experience that you can only get whilst sailing!
Are there any routes or itineraries that are especially popular with clients at present, or any you are looking to promote?
We have seen growth in our mile-builder trips, which offer more days and nights at sea to gain experience. From hopping over the English Channel to sailing the length of the UK and crossing the Atlantic, time away from it all with no digital connection is becoming more popular. For some, it’s an alternative to travelling to different locations without flying. Sustainable slow travel wasn’t a major consideration five years ago, but it has become a factor in people’s decision-making when planning a holiday. Solo travellers now make up 40% of our customer base, with equal proportions of men and women joining our boats alone. There is definitely a specific type of customer that joins our trips, so you are bound to meet like-minded people.
How do you see the state of the industry at present? Are there reasons to be cheerful?
There is an increasing amount of legislation changes from our own MCA and other countries’ marine authorities. Sailing in some areas of Europe and the world has become a paperwork nightmare for charter sector operators, and it’s getting increasingly difficult for the historic fleet to make ends meet with so many cost increases, which is worrying for their futures. But we see nothing but growth in our little corner of the market, as people want to escape the ordinary and use their well-earned time off to try something completely different that’s perhaps out of their comfort zone.

Many Colours of the Sea: A Sailor’s Perspective of the Ocean’s Changing Tones
Spend any time afloat and you soon realise that the sea presents itself in many different moods - and colour is an important aspect of this as Jess explains.
Author
:
Jess Lloyd-Mostyn
Published Date:
10 February 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
colours of the sea  |  Photo credit: Holly Astle
Nothing can really prepare you for how completely your relationship with the water changes when you’re living on it. From vibrant ceruleans and cobalts to bleached-out leaden greys; vivid azures of the sandy shallows to dark navys and indigos of the deep sea, all of them become a familiar backdrop to the cruising life.
Before we moved aboard to sail full-time I had lived in London and acknowledged, in passing, the churning brown tidal activity of the Thames. At university I was based in Brighton and would pick my way across its pebbly beach to get closer to the crashing waves or else gaze out at a pink and orange-streaked sunset reflected in the sea below.
But those seem like almost passing acquaintances compared with the sea that I know now so intimately, with all of its different moods and in a whole spectrum of assorted shades.
Turquoise Sea
For example, there is the particular type of turquoise that I associate with shallow, tropical water, over a sand sea floor. This is the kind of water that makes Brits in winter swoon and keeps postcard companies in business. When I reflect on my encounters with this colour specifically, I can instantly conjure up memories from cruising in the
Tuamotus
or Bora Bora in French Polynesia, lazily hammock swaying on deck in St Lucia or the British Virgin islands in the Caribbean, and paradisical remote and unspoilt beaches in the Fijian islands, Raja Ampat in Indonesia and parts of Thailand. It’s a hue that feels very far away from anything in my European experiences, although I’m certain that you can still find it there. So, for me, it’s become almost symbolic of the exotic, of places and countries that I’ve had to sail significant miles, and crossed oceans to get to and play in.
Then there is a deep, ultramarine blue that seems to be expansive and fathomless. It’s dark and you can feel a coolness from it, even in the tropics. Unlike the swimming-pool clarity of that turquoise, this blue gives away none of its secrets and could hide anything within its incomprehensible depths. Yet, it’s also a tone that I associate with adventure and anticipation and seems to be the very reason that our boat has the phrase “bluewater” connected to her. This is the blue of deep, ocean sailing. Not just a jolly, not just a day sail, this is the colour of a voyage, one that sees the crew travelling upon it safely across to another continent.
“Sea green” is a rather elastic term, as there are so many greeny-blues in different types of water but I tend to link it to areas where the water is fairly clear but with a grass bottom. This is also just the kind of environment which attracts interesting creatures such as turtles and dugongs, who love to eat from the grassy floor. This colour reminds me of slowly
paddleboarding
, gently gliding across the surface, while peering down and looking for a flash of flipper or catching a glimpse of a slightly brown shell, and then easing into the water with my snorkel and mask to witness the turtles dance and feed, so otherworldly and serene. I would be overcome with an immense swelling feeling of pleasure and gratitude while hanging still in their greenish home.
Stormy Colours of the Sea
Above the surface, at other times, the sky darkens rapidly as a squall approaches or a storm starts to brew. Where the glowering clouds meet the water it turns a peculiar shade of light aquamarine blue, almost illuminated from within, and a stark contrast to the threatening sky visible above. This streak of blue looks almost neon, like a highlighter pen, and seems oddly hopeful, as if it promises of fine conditions to come again once the chaos of the ominous weather has passed.
In the rain both the sky and the sea are matched in a steely grey tone. The contrast disappears and up ahead is nothing but a foggy wall of a total whiteout. The sea’s surface is splattered and textured with the constant droplets. Yet, it’s a colour that I associate with snuggling up together in the cabin below. I have so many memories of looking out at the water while wrapped up warm and cosy, with a mug of tea in my hand, and catching glances of the grey sea and rain from one of the portlights or the windows of the cockpit tent.
From seeing dolphins burst out of the lazuline depths to spotting fishing canoes in the monochrome of the dawn light the ocean has displayed a full palette of feelings to us. And I will never lose the sense of wonder at our great fortune to live among them.

“It Was Shifty, Wacky Conditions, Like Mario Kart Out There” - Australians Win SailGP in Auckland, Taking Over Emirates GBR
It was the second weekend of the SailGP 2026 calendar and the F50s foiling around Auckland Harbour did not disappoint. Here is the full report from the ITM New Zealand Sail Grand Prix, including details on the catastrophic crash.
Author
:
Milly Karsten, Digital Editor
Published Date:
16 February 2026
SHARE THIS ARTICLE
Auckland SailGP  |  Photo credit: Felix Diemer for SailGP
After the excitement of the opening 2026 SailGP weekend, the Oracle Perth Sail Grand Prix presented by KPMG back in January, SailGP has delivered yet again another dramatic weekend of high-speed racing on the foiling F50s in Auckland.
From 14th - 15th February, Auckland Harbour was brought alive with the ITM New Zealand Sail Grand Prix. It was a much anticipated weekend in the
SailGP 2026 schedule
, with great numbers of fans ascending on the harbour and watching from home.
After a sobering crash on the first day, the fleet regrouped and managed to stay focused, racing hard over the weekend and adding excitement to the 2026 SailGP leaderboards.
SailGP Auckland - Emirates GBR F50  |  Photo credit: Jason Ludlow for SailGP
Huge Crash Between the Kiwi and French Boats
Crowds and shore teams were left stunned on Saturday when high-speed foiling down the first leg of the third race resulted in a
catastrophic SailGP crash between the French and Kiwi boats
. Both boats sustained major damage and two SailGP athletes were rushed to hospital for medical attention.
This was a huge blow to the Kiwi team, after only just repairing their F50 from a crash with the Swiss team on the Perth SailGP weekend in January.
Sir Russell Coutts, SailGP co-founder and CEO had commented before the Auckland weekend: “It has been a monumental effort to get them back on the start line, and I’m sure the New Zealand fans will turn out in full force to support the Black Foils this weekend.”
SailGP Auckland  |  Photo credit: Andrew Baker for SailGP
Auckland SailGP Results and Race Report
Nevertheless, the remainder of the SailGP fleet battled it out in Auckland Harbour over the weekend. Auckland is one of the closest racetracks on the SailGP 2026 calendar, and this year they experienced gusts up to 35 km/h, making for some exciting and challenging racing.
On Saturday, the Bonds Flying Roos repeated history, taking victory on the opening fleet race of the day, with some challenge from the Kiwi boat before their unfortunate crash.
Defending Rolex SailGP Champions Emirates Great Britain had a mixed first day, with driver
Dylan Fletcher
telling reporters, “It was very difficult conditions, but to be honest, it was a lot of fun until the incident. We had a terrible first race, didn’t sail very well. We finally got our act together, and felt like we were coming good. It was shifty, wacky conditions, like Mario Kart out there.”
After the crash on the first day, the format for day 2 changed to a split fleet. As the rain poured down, adding to the drama and suspense, the Australian team fought off Emirates GBR and Spain, securing their second consecutive victory in Auckland.
The Australian’s win now takes them above Emirates GBR on the overall standings, tied on points (19 each) with Emirates GBR.
SailGP 2026 leaderboard after Auckland  |  Photo credit: SailGP
More on the Emirates GBR
Looking for more news on the British SailGP team? Take a look at our
exclusive interview with Dylan Fletcher at the end of the last SailGP season
.

Ikonic Yachts partners with Lekker Boats for representation in American market
19 February 2026 •
by Emily Dawkins
Miami-based advisory firm
Ikonic Yachts
has announced a new partnership with Dutch boat builder Lekker Boats to represent the brand's Lekker 44 and Lekker 45 dayboats throughout the US.
The new collaboration marks the builder's official move into the American market, bringing its aluminium boat designs from Europe to US buyers for the first time.
Ikonic Yachts will present the 13.5-metre Lekker 44 and the new 13.7-metre build, the Lekker 45, to the market. Both boats are constructed in lightweight aluminium, the latter of which is presented as a "significant evolution" of the brand's platform, offering 20 per cent more interior space than its predecessor and a larger, 1.6-metre swim platform.
The Lekker 45
The Lekker 44
"We are thrilled to partner with Lekker Boats and bring their exceptional vessels to the American market," said AJ Blackmon, CEO of Ikonic Yachts. "Lekker has built an outstanding reputation in Europe for creating aluminium performance dayboats that perfectly blend speed, comfort and stunning design. These boats are ideally suited for the South Florida and Bahamas market, where their shallow draft, fuel efficiency and entertaining-focused layouts really shine."
Blackmon continued: "We're also seeing tremendous interest from the superyacht community, as the Lekker 44 and 45 make exceptional yacht tenders. They're fast enough to keep up with motherships, tough enough for daily use and stylish enough to complement any luxury yacht. This partnership allows us to offer our clients something truly unique in the American market."
Meindert Wolfraad, founder of Lekker Boats, added: “What makes this partnership special is the genuine alignment in how we think about boating and the people we serve. As a boutique shipyard producing a limited number of boats each year, we prioritise a personal, design-led approach tailored to each owner. Ikonic brings that same down-to-earth energy and individualised service to their brokerage, and the connection between our teams felt natural from the very beginning. It was immediately clear that we share the same philosophy and energy when it comes to boating and client relationships.”
The Lekker 45 is scheduled for delivery in October 2025, while the Lekker 44 is presented as the 2024 unit,
Parallax
.

Nominations open for The Honours 2026 by the Superyacht Life Foundation
23 February 2026
• Written by Holly Margerrison
Nominations have opened for The Honours 2026, an initiative by the Superyacht Life Foundation (SYLF) that recognises individuals making a tangible impact across the superyacht sector.
Now in its fourth year, The Honours highlights people whose work is reshaping perceptions of the industry, both within yachting and beyond. Previous editions have celebrated a broad mix of figures, including ocean conservationists, founders of philanthropic projects and advocates for diversity, education and scientific research.
The awards are open to nominations from across the industry spectrum, from crew and captains to designers, engineers, owners, philanthropists and researchers. Candidates may be put forward for initiatives spanning sustainability, mentorship, charitable work or pioneering technical and design projects.
An independent panel comprising industry and non-industry representatives will review submissions. Three honourees will be selected following the judging process, with further details on the announcement to be released in due course.
“The Honours is about giving these exceptional people a voice and showing the world the incredible impact they are making. We encourage the entire community to come forward and nominate the people who inspire them,” says Dilan Saraç, executive director of SYLF.
Nominations can be submitted via
thehonours.org
.

57m Codecasa yacht Legacy 4 launched
23 February 2026
• Written by Gemma Harris and Emily Dawkins
Italian shipyard
Codecasa
has announced the launch of its 56.8-metre new build superyacht, now christened
Legacy 4
. The first Codecasa 57 unit, known as hull F81 throughout construction, hit the water at a private ceremony at the Codecasa Ugo Shipyards in Viareggio.
Little has been revealed about the project since construction began in December 2023, though it was known that she would feature a steel hull and an aluminium superstructure built to Lloyd's Register classification.
Codecasa have now announced a collaboration with Italian designer
Tommaso Spadolini
for the new build, with the teams developing the exterior and interior designs of
Legacy 4
to offer a balance between privacy and conviviality.
The yacht was last seen in 2024 en route to outfitting
Accommodation will be across an owner's suite and five guest cabins, in addition to a captain's cabin and six twin cabins for the crew. An elevator will also connect all decks.
Powered by twin 2,375hp Caterpillar engines,
Legacy 4
is expected to reach a maximum speed of 17 knots at light displacement, with a range of 5,000 nautical miles when pared back to 12 knots. All other details remain undisclosed.
The yacht began construction at the yard's facilities in Pisa but was later transported to Viareggio
A statement from the yard read: "The launch of the
Legacy 4
is the result of the hard teamwork, expertise, passion and dedication of the whole Codecasa Shipyards team. To all Codecasa people –from the design to the construction, up to the final installations – goes our heartfelt thanks for the commitment and professionalism they have devoted to this project." Special thanks also went to the subcontractors, the owning company and Yanova and
Fraser
, who were advisors during the build.
According to
BOATPro
, the shipyard currently has four yachts under construction, including the seventh unit from its Codecasa 43 yacht model, which is scheduled for delivery in spring 2028.

First look inside final 80m Bilgin 263 superyacht Al Reem
23 February 2026
• Written by Dea Jusufi and Emily Dawkins
Turkish shipyard
Bilgin Yachts
has offered a first look inside the third and final unit from its Bilgin 263 series,
Al Reem
.
The previously secretive 80.1-metre motor yacht – aptly known as Project Silence throughout construction – was delivered to her owners in July 2025 and has since spent a "successful" summer season cruising the Turkish coast before passing Egypt and cruising the Red Sea.
She builds on the success of the first unit in the series,
Tatiana
, which made her global debut at the 2021
Monaco Yacht Show
as the largest yacht ever built in Turkey. The second unit,
Leona
, was delivered in 2023 and won at the
BOAT International Design & Innovation Awards
for her outstanding exterior design. All three hulls in the 263 series share a dynamic, slender profile and naval architecture created by
Unique Yacht Design
, with interiors penned by British studio
H2 Yacht Design
.
According to the yard, however,
Al Reem
represents the "most highly customised" of the line, and offers almost 100GT more volume – including 20 per cent more exterior decking. She is said to embody her owner's strict philosophy of “less is more," with the yard noting how the yacht set a new benchmark for "capacity, performance and bespoke craftsmanship within the series".
Images reveal the superyacht's bright and contemporary colour palette, characterised by tones of cream, white and beige to create a "vast and open" atmosphere. Organic matt timbers, polished stainless steel, natural stones and leathers complete the feeling of a "calm, tactile luxury".
To eliminate visual barriers in the main saloon, the usual bar area has been removed and the entertainment systems hidden, allowing for views from the aft through to the custom waterfall which cascades into the foredeck jacuzzi. In place of a bar, the main staircase area, which leads to the beach club, has instead been dedicated to a bespoke art installation. The yacht is described as a "subtly moving gallery", with a number of carefully selected artworks on board.
The superyacht has a foredeck crafted entirely from teak, alongside a superstructure designed in "a striking combination of metallic grey-white and black". She also benefits from a fresh new layout with larger living areas and a number of technical refinements, including a longer upper deck and an extra covered area at the stern.
The owner's deck, for example, is positioned on the main deck for more privacy and designed with 300-degree views and an outdoor terrace and Jacuzzi area. The remaining guest accommodation comprises two VIPs on the main deck and three guest cabins on the lower deck. An elevator connects all decks.
The beach club is also a highlight on board, featuring an opening "shell door" that serves as a private massage area, alongside a dedicated steam room and an integrated sauna in the well-equipped gym. Another customised feature is the advanced sound insulation onboard and the stabiliser-equipped bed in the master suite.
On the technical side,
Al Reem
has a new ventilation system and an increased generator capacity. The yacht can cruise at a maximum speed of 19 knots, powered by a pair of MTU 16V 4000M73 engines, with an economical range of 5,000 nautical miles.
13 images
Al Reem
(right) pictured with the 49-metre Bilgin 163 yacht
Camila
“To conclude the 263 trilogy with such a uniquely personalised vessel – one that has already garnered technical awards for its complexity and quality – is a testament to our shipyard’s capability to deliver the owner's most demanding visions for tranquillity and luxury," said Ismail Şengün, CEO of Bilgin Yachts at
Al Reem
's delivery.
A statement from the owner read: "
Al Reem
is the realisation of the owner’s vision for tranquillity, art  and uncompromising comfort on the water – a minimalist masterpiece in the world of luxury yachting."
Al Reem
was launched in December 2024, first sold in 2022 to a buyer represented by
Bluewater
.
According to
BOATPro
, Bilgin Yachts has 14 yachts on order or actively under construction, including seven units from the 52-metre Bilgin 170 series. Bilgin Yachts celebrated the launch of its second 52-metre Bilgin 170 yacht, NB83, in December.

/

Designer Remi Tessier and WhatsApp co-founder Jan Koum reach settlement in dispute
19 February 2026 •
by BOAT International
French designer
Remi Tessier
and his firm, Dernier Etage (DES), have reached a confidential commercial settlement with WhatsApp co-founder Jan Koum in a high-profile dispute concerning the latter's luxury property and two superyachts.
In court documents filed in October to the Southern District court of New York, Tessier's legal team moved to dismiss Koum's application for US discovery, calling it a "retributive campaign" to damage Tessier's reputation and breach the confidentiality of ongoing arbitrations in London and Paris.

RMK Marine expands yacht building facilities with new 70,000 sqm shipyard in Yalova, Turkey
19 February 2026 •
by Emily Dawkins
Turkish builder
RMK Marine
has announced that it has begun construction on a new shipyard in Yalova, Turkey, dedicated entirely to its yacht-building brand, RMK Yachts. The new site will feature 70,000 square metres of yacht construction facilities at the water's edge and is scheduled to have full operational capacity by 2027.

UJ & UQJ Kits
Offering rapid turnaround
on UJ & UQJ kits and piece parts
SEA TALK SYSTEMS ARE THE INDEPENDENT APPROVED SUPPLIER OF UJ AND UQJ KITS, EQUIPMENT AND TOOL KITS TO THE UJ CONSORTIUM MEMBERS
STS support ALL kits for UJ, UQJ, Training Kits, Ferrule Kits, Tool Kits, Piece Parts and Equipment.
Since inception STS have inspected and supplied in excess of:
140,000 UJ/UQJ kits
5 million piece parts
2,500 tool kits
…On time, with zero defects and full traceability!
RAPID TURNAROUND
RAPID TURNAROUND
Sea Talk currently provides a standard 5 week lead time on most of all the high demand kits and standard products. This requires the holding of at least £2m worth of strategic stock and a range of individual piece parts that can be quickly converted into kits.
Working in partnership
Working in partnership
STS work in partnership with individual UJ Consortium Members by holding an agreed level of complete kits for call off when required.
Due to Sea Talks close working relationship with UJ Consortium Members, STS can offer a rapid turnaround on kits and piece parts to support emergency requirements.

Cookies Policy
In this policy, unless it is obvious that they have some other meaning, the words "we", "our" and "us" refers to
www.seatalk.com
and its owner(s) Sea Talk Systems Ltd.
To contact the data protection officer you should email
[email protected]
.
This Cookie Policy outlines to you what “cookies” are, why they are used, how we use them and how you can disable them in your browser if desired. Please be aware that if you disable cookies in your browser there may be areas and aspects of our website which will not work as expected.
This cookie policy should be read alongside our
privacy policy
so you fully understand how and why we use cookies and the additional information we may collect from you. If you do not fully understand and agree with our cookie and privacy policies you must cease use of our website immediately.
If you require any clarification about our cookie policy please email the data protection officer.
Cookies – what are they?
Cookies are small text files which are stored on your computer’s hard drive. They can be used by websites to track the user, remember preferences, allow the user to login to secure areas of websites, store items in virtual shopping baskets and a whole host of other tasks.
Why and how do we use Cookies?
We use cookies to allow for a greater browsing experience for our users. As illustrated above, Cookies can perform a number of very useful tasks which allow this. We may also use Cookies to help us better understand our users, how they use our website and their preferences to further enhance our website and business.
The Cookies we may use tend to fit into three main categories. A list of the three categories follows, along with an explanation of how and why we use Cookies in each instance.
Analytical Cookies
We may use anonymous analytics Cookies to track the number of users we have, the pages they visit, the duration of the visit, and a whole host of other useful information about how our users browse and engage our website.
Knowing this information allows us to understand our audience reach, audience demographics, the success our marketing campaigns, which areas of our website are most popular, and clearer picture of how to design and implement new changes to our website and business going forward. We are not able to identify you as an individual using analytics Cookies.
Functionality Cookies
We may use Cookies as a method of storing information which allows you to browse and use our website efficiently.
We may use such Cookies to perform tasks such as storing the items you have added to your online shopping cart, the information you filled into a form, your credentials to keep you logged into a secure area of the website, and to remember various other preferences.
Cookies from third-parties
The Web is an integrated place. Like most other websites, we may have third-party applications, programs, widgets and material on our website. Some of these may use Cookies to track various metrics, store preferences and customise their material to you.
An example of this which is widely used across the Web is social media buttons which allow users to like, share and comment about what they find on websites and may result in Cookies being set.
We cannot view or change third-party cookies, in the same way third-parties cannot view or change cookies set by us.
Turning Cookies off in your browser
If you decide to turn Cookies off in your browser you should be aware that certain areas and features of our website may not work as expected.
The majority of mainstream, up-to-date browsers allow you to disabled Cookies. Usually you can find where to do this by going to the options or preference tab within your browser. We offer some links below which may help you:
Firefox:
Enable and disable cookies that websites use to track your preferences
.
Internet Explorer:
Block or allow cookies
.
Google Chrome:
Enable or disable cookies
.
For users worried about Cookies used by advertisers, you can learn much more about them and how to disable them by visit the
Your Online Choices
.
You can email the data protection officer if you require any further information about Cookies.

Sea Talk
海底産業への
接続技術
不良ゼロで
納期通りの
納品実績
Sea Talk Systems は、UJ および UQJ キット、装置、ツールキットを UJ Consortiumメンバーに供給する独立認定サプライヤーです。
Sea Talk Systems は、海底ケーブル業界のキットおよびピースパーツの供給において数十年の経験を有する、密接に連携したチームメンバーで構成されるプロのチームです。
チームは100%信頼できるピースパーツ、キット、装置の重要性、そして膨大な故障コストを理解しています。これこそチームが故障リスクを排除するために厳格なプロセスを使用する理由であり、その実績はチーム全体の入念な作業の証となっています。
トレーニングはビジネスの中心に据えられており、STS はチームが最も正確な技術と最高の装置を使用していることを保証しています。チーム全員が複数のスキルを有し、この柔軟性により、注文を納期通りに納品する必要がある場合には、いつでもその業務に集中します。
経営陣はプロセスすべてを請け負う方法に関するトレーニングを受けています。このトレーニングにより、信頼性と効率性の両方において改善を行い、また潜在的なリスクを特定し、これを軽減するための識見を得ることができます。ビジネスのあらゆる局面をごく細部にいたるまで理解することは、100%の信頼性と納期通りの納品実績を維持する上で鍵となります。
品質管理
品質管理
UJ および UQJ キット
UJ および UQJ キット
お問い合わせ
お問い合わせ
STS は全数検査のビジネスモデルを使って事業を運営しています。これは事業を Cable & Wireless Marine から STS へ移管した際に引き継いだ慣行です。このビジネスモデルを維持することで、品質が非常に重要視される業界においてキットおよびピースパーツの品質と不良ゼロ確保が継続します。
この業界においては、システムダウンした場合、一つ故障が起きただけで、1日に何千万もの収益損となることがあります。もちろんトラフィックのリルーティングコスト、ケーブル敷設船の緊急レンタル、接続人員、評判や顧客信頼へのダメージは言うに及びません。
通常、システム故障の主な原因は漁船、船の錨、地震、津波、潮汐水です。STS は、修理や新しいキットが必要な場合、不良ゼロの目的に適合した製品やサービスを適時に供給することを保証することを誇りとしています。
STS は最新の装置やプロセス管理を用いて、最先端の専用施設で業務を行います。
Sea Talk の業務の概要をご説明したビデオをご覧ください
。

Privacy Policy
We are committed to safeguarding your privacy. This Policy explains how we collect and use personal information.
Please read the following carefully to understand our views and practices regarding your personal data and how we will treat it. By visiting
www.seatalk.com
you are accepting and consenting to the practices described in this policy.
For the purpose of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) (GDPR), the data controller is Gary Ritchie.
To contact the data protection officer you should email
[email protected]
.
References to ‘we’, ‘our’ and ‘us’ within this Privacy Policy are to Sea Talk Systems Ltd.
The information we collect
This Policy applies to information you give us, through our website or by corresponding with us and information collected by us as you visit or interact with our website.
The data you provide may include:
Your name
Your email
Your contact details
The data we collect about you may include, but is not limited to:
Your IP address
Information about your browser and operating system
Information about your visit, this may include, but is not limited to, pages looked at, visit duration, errors encountered and interaction with the pages.
How we use your information
Any personal information we collect from this website is used in accordance with the General Data Protection Regulation (EU) 2016/679) and other applicable laws.
The information you provide allows us to do the following:
If you have made an enquiry then we will use your contact details to respond to your enquiry.
If you opted in to our newsletter then we send you our newsletter.
To provide you with the information, products and services that you request from us.
To notify you about changes to our services.
To protect keep our website safe and secure.
To improve our website and personalise it for you and your computer.
To provide an efficient service, certain functions such as hosting, storage and disaster recovery are provided by third parties.
To provide interactive features.
To measure the effectiveness of our advertising.
It is our policy to ensure that your personal data is not made accessible to unauthorised individuals. We have taken reasonable steps to protect both the digital and physical storage of data.
We strive to protect your personal information, we cannot ensure or warrant the security of any information you transmit to us and you do so at your own risk.
Your data may be kept if it is required for legal or accounting reasons.
Disclosure of your information
Analytics and search engines providers that assist us in the improvement and optimisation of our site may collect data about your IP and computer set up.
How long do we keep your data
We will not retain your personal information longer than necessary.
If legally required or if it is reasonably necessary to meet regulatory requirements, resolve disputes, prevent fraud and abuse, or enforce our Terms and Conditions, we may also retain some of your information for a limited period of time as required, even after it is no longer needed to provide services to you.
Cookies
Cookies are small pieces of information that are stored by your browser on your device's hard-drive. They are used to distinguish individual users, and help us improve our website.
We explain how we use cookies in our
cookies policy
.
Access to your personal information
If you would like to update or remove the data we have about you please submit a request to the data protection officer.
If you wish to unsubscribe from our email newsletter you should use the unsubscribe link at the bottom of the email. Should this not work please contact the data protection officer, using the details at the start of this policy.
If you would like to request access to the data we have about you please submit a request to the data protection officer.
If an access request is deemed by us to be unfounded or excessive we may charge a reasonable fee. If you request further copies of your data then we may charge for administrative fees.
Complaints
If you wish to make a complaint about our use of your data please contact the data protection officer.
Updates to this policy
This Policy was last updated in March 2018.
Any changes we make to this policy will be posted to this page.

SEA TALK SYSTEMS ARE THE INDEPENDENT APPROVED SUPPLIER OF UJ AND UQJ KITS, EQUIPMENT AND TOOL KITS TO THE UJ CONSORTIUM MEMBERS
Sea Talk Systems are a close knit team of professionals, most with decades of experience in the supply of kits and piece parts for the submarine cable industry.
They understand the importance of 100% reliable piece parts, kits and equipment, as well as the enormous costs of failure. This is why they operate strict processes to eliminate the risk of failures, and their track record stands as testament to the careful work of the entire team.
Training is at the core of the business, STS make sure their team is using the most accurate techniques and the best equipment. All of their team are multi-skilled; this flexibility allows them to direct their efforts to wherever it is needed to fulfil their orders on time.
The management team are trained in how to undertake all of their processes. This gives them the insight to make improvements to both reliability and efficiency as well as spotting possible risks to mitigate. Understanding every aspect of the business in intimate detail is key to maintaining their track record of 100% reliability, delivered on time.
Quality Control
Quality Control
UJ & UQJ Kits
UJ & UQJ Kits
Contact
Contact
STS operate using a 100% inspection business model, which they inherited when the business was transferred from Cable & Wireless Marine to STS. Maintaining this business model continues to ensure quality and defect free kits and piece parts in this quality critical industry.
A single failure in this industry can result in the loss of hundreds of thousands in lost revenue per day if your system is down. Not to mention the cost of re-routing traffic, the emergency hire of cable ships, jointing personnel, damage to your reputation and customers’ confidence.
Typically the main reasons for system failures can be fishing boats, ship anchors, earthquakes, tsunamis and tidal waters. STS are proud to guarantee that when repairs, or new kits are necessary, they will be supplied on time with zero defects and fit for purpose.
STS operate from a state of the art, purpose built facility, using the latest equipment and process controls.

for a summarised view of Sea Talk’s operations.

S
ea

alk

ystems have a wealth of experience and proven track record for providing quality controlled kits to the UJ Consortium for over 28 years.
Sea Talk Systems business model is 100% inspection of piece parts, kits and equipment.
STS maintain a flexible approach to meeting all their customers’ requirements but will not compromise on quality. They focus on avoiding failure and its consequences.
Fast and accurate
Fast and accurate
Dimensional checking needs to be both fast and accurate. STS has invested in the latest co-ordinate measuring machines to maintain their confidence in the conformance of components to specification. All their inspection equipment is located in dedicated temperature controlled environments to aid in achieving these high standards.
Certified Quality
Certified Quality
STS holds
ISO 9001:2015 QUALITY CERTIFICATION
and also require their manufacturing subcontractors to maintain this accreditation as a minimum. Rigorous internal and external audits are carried out and continuously monitored and improved.
The established processes and practices at STS that have been developed within a business environment, demand a formal quality assurance regime and full traceability of activities, processes and materials.
Sourcing and procurement
Sourcing and procurement
Sea Talk’s role is to provide a sourcing and procurement facility to supply parts which range from small to high precision mechanical piece parts to equipment, at competitive costs and to the highest quality.
Every manufactured part, without exception, is subjected to a 100% inspection process, which includes dimensional checks, hardness tests, threads and finish, to drawing requirements. All mechanical equipment such as moulds and hydraulic presses are assembled and verified prior to despatch. Jointing equipment is fully assembled and checked prior to despatch.
Supplier management
Supplier management
A list of approved suppliers is maintained from information obtained from supplier questionnaires, historic data and delivery / quality performance. New suppliers are only added following successful audit assessment and prove out of manufacturing processes.
STS electronically store all inspection and manufacturing records to ensure there is full traceability of kits and piece parts for 25 years. In the event of system fault, without full traceability, it will be impossible to identify the root cause and take corrective action.

A proven track

SEA TALK SYSTEMS ARE THE INDEPENDENT APPROVED SUPPLIER OF UJ AND UQJ KITS, EQUIPMENT AND TOOL KITS TO THE UJ CONSORTIUM MEMBERS
Sea Talk Systems are a close knit team of professionals, most with decades of experience in the supply of kits and piece parts for the submarine cable industry.
They understand the importance of 100% reliable piece parts, kits and equipment, as well as the enormous costs of failure. This is why they operate strict processes to eliminate the risk of failures, and their track record stands as testament to the careful work of the entire team.
Training is at the core of the business, STS make sure their team is using the most accurate techniques and the best equipment. All of their team are multi-skilled; this flexibility allows them to direct their efforts to wherever it is needed to fulfil their orders on time.
The management team are trained in how to undertake all of their processes. This gives them the insight to make improvements to both reliability and efficiency as well as spotting possible risks to mitigate. Understanding every aspect of the business in intimate detail is key to maintaining their track record of 100% reliability, delivered on time.
Quality Control
Quality Control
UJ & UQJ Kits
UJ & UQJ Kits
Contact
Contact
STS operate using a 100% inspection business model, which they inherited when the business was transferred from Cable & Wireless Marine to STS. Maintaining this business model continues to ensure quality and defect free kits and piece parts in this quality critical industry.
A single failure in this industry can result in the loss of hundreds of thousands in lost revenue per day if your system is down. Not to mention the cost of re-routing traffic, the emergency hire of cable ships, jointing personnel, damage to your reputation and customers’ confidence.
Typically the main reasons for system failures can be fishing boats, ship anchors, earthquakes, tsunamis and tidal waters. STS are proud to guarantee that when repairs, or new kits are necessary, they will be supplied on time with zero defects and fit for purpose.
STS operate from a state of the art, purpose built facility, using the latest equipment and process controls.

for a summarised view of Sea Talk’s operations.

Our Processes
SEA TALK SYSTEMS ARE THE INDEPENDENT APPROVED SUPPLIER OF UJ AND UQJ KITS, EQUIPMENT AND TOOL KITS TO THE UJ CONSORTIUM MEMBERS
Sea Talk Systems run all operations using a bespoke Process Control Plan with the end goal in mind. Their system guarantees that STS start right to finish right and provide their Customers with quality products on time with zero defects.
STS work in partnership with individual UJ Consortium Members by holding an agreed level of complete kits for call off when required.
Due to Sea Talk's close working relationship with it’s UJ Consortium Members, STS can offer a rapid turnaround on kits and piece parts to support emergency requirements.
Each kit is uniquely serialised to ensure full traceability from top level to each individual part within the kit. Each kit is given a double and independent check to ensure that all kits are supplied with zero defects.
